c95e97c9e4f03e3cf7c03cbe8bd72efe4e02efcc.text: Based on the provided documentation, the limits and quotas for AWS Fargate (which is used with Amazon EKS) are as follows: 1. Fargate Resource rate quota for On Demand Amazon ECS tasks and Amazon EKS pods [Source: aws/ECS/Amazon ECS service quotas > AWS Fargate service quotas]: - Bucket maximum capacity (or Burst rate): 100 - Bucket refill rate (or Sustained rate): 20 2. The tasks and pods token bucket size for a Fargate customer account is also 100 tokens, and the refill rate is 20 tokens per second [Source: aws/ECS/AWS Fargate throttling quotas]. 3. For Amazon EKS pods launched using specific platform versions, there is a burst rate of 20 with a sustained pod launch rate of 20 pod launches per second [Source: aws/ECS/AWS Fargate throttling quotas > Fargate Resource rate quota for On Demand Amazon ECS tasks and Amazon EKS pods]. 4. The rate quota for calls to the Amazon ECS `RunTask` API is 20 calls per second (burst and sustained) [Source: aws/ECS/AWS Fargate throttling quotas > Throttling the `RunTask` API]. Each call to this API can launch up to 10 tasks.

508bb6bb11d8d2c6c2cfea6830b3b500635f2675.text: AWS VPC (Virtual Private Cloud) is a service provided by Amazon Web Services that allows users to launch AWS resources in a virtual network that they define. The main features of an AWS VPC include the ability to configure IP address ranges, create subnets, and configure route tables, network gateways, and security settings [Source: aws/EC2/Virtual private clouds]. By default, a VPC is created in each Region with a default subnet for each Availability Zone, an internet gateway attached to the VPC, and a route in the main route table that sends all traffic (0.0.0.0/0) to the internet gateway [Source: aws/EC2/Virtual private clouds > Your default VPCs]. Instances launched into a default subnet have access to the internet due to this configuration. Users can also create their own VPC and configure it to meet their specific needs [Source: aws/EC2/Virtual private clouds > Your default VPCs]. In addition, Dedicated Instances support several features and AWS service integrations when launched into a VPC, such as Reserved Instances, Automatic scaling, Automatic recovery, Dedicated Spot Instances, and Burstable performance instances [Source: aws/EC2/Dedicated Instances > Supported features]. Furthermore, Amazon VPC flow logs can be used to monitor the performance of Elastic Fabric Adapters by capturing information about the traffic going to and from an EFA [Source: aws/EC2/Monitor an EFA > Amazon VPC flow logs]. Lastly, ClassicLink is a feature that allows EC2-Classic instances to be linked to a VPC and access resources within it, but resources on the other side of a VPN connection or an Internet gateway do not have access to a linked EC2-Classic instance [Source: aws/EC2/ClassicLink > Work with ClassicLink].

2680cde7231f31a2b3117943b252068b54cdf245.text: Based on the provided documentation, the limits and quotas for AWS ECS can be found in two sections: Amazon ECS service quotas and AWS Fargate service quotas. 1. Default service quotas for Amazon ECS are listed in [Source: aws/ECS/Amazon ECS service quotas]. These quotas include limits for tasks, services, clusters, and other resources. For more details, please refer to the documentation at . 2. AWS Fargate service quotas are listed under the **AWS Fargate** service in the Service Quotas console [Source: aws/ECS/Amazon ECS service quotas > AWS Fargate service quotas]. These quotas include limits for tasks, services, and other resources specific to Fargate. For more details, please refer to the documentation at . It is important to note that new AWS accounts might have initial lower quotas that can increase over time [Source: aws/ECS/Amazon ECS service quotas > AWS Fargate service quotas]. Additionally, Fargate enforces Amazon ECS tasks and Amazon EKS pods launch rate limits [Source: aws/ECS/Amazon ECS service quotas > AWS Fargate service quotas]. For more information about these throttling limits, please refer to the documentation at . To view and manage these service quotas in the AWS Management Console, follow the instructions provided in [Source: aws/ECS/Amazon ECS service quotas > Managing your Amazon ECS and AWS Fargate service quotas in the AWS Management Console].

AWS IAM (Identity and Access Management) is a service provided by Amazon Web Services that enables you to securely control access to your AWS resources. The main features of AWS IAM, as described in the context, include:

1. Identity-based policies: Yes [Source: aws/ECS/How Amazon Elastic Container Service works with IAM]
2. Policy actions: Yes [Source: aws/ECS/How Amazon Elastic Container Service works with IAM]
3. Policy condition keys: Yes [Source: aws/ECS/How Amazon Elastic Container Service works with IAM]
4. ABAC (tags in policies): Yes [Source: aws/ECS/How Amazon Elastic Container Service works with IAM]
5. Temporary credentials: Yes [Source: aws/ECS/How Amazon Elastic Container Service works with IAM]
6. Principal permissions: Yes [Source: aws/ECS/How Amazon Elastic Container Service works with IAM]
7. Service roles: Yes [Source: aws/ECS/How Amazon Elastic Container Service works with IAM]

For more information about AWS IAM, you can refer to the [AWS IAM User Guide](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/IAM/latest/UserGuide/what-is-iam.html).

cd40953dc062b25a6f20cc3e599b2a186d980e24.text: The provided context outlines various limits and quotas for AWS EC2 services. Here's a summary of some key limits and quotas based on the context: 1. On-Demand Instance quotas: You can view your current quotas using the Service Quotas console or the AWS Trusted Advisor console [Source: aws/EC2/On\-Demand Instances > On\-Demand Instance quotas > Monitor On\-Demand Instance quotas and usage] - To view your current quotas using the Service Quotas console: a. Open the Service Quotas console at [https://console.aws.amazon.com/servicequotas/home/services/ec2/quotas/](https://console.aws.amazon.com/servicequotas/home/services/ec2/quotas/) b. From the navigation bar, select a Region c. In the filter field, enter **On-Demand** d. The **Applied quota value** column displays the maximum number of vCPUs for each On-Demand Instance quota type for your account 2. Amazon EBS quotas: To view the quotas for your Amazon EBS resources, open the Service Quotas console at [https://console.aws.amazon.com/servicequotas/](https://console.aws.amazon.com/servicequotas/) [Source: aws/EC2/Amazon EBS quotas] 3. Amazon EC2 service quotas: Amazon EC2 provides different resources that you can use, including images, instances, volumes, and snapshots. When you create your AWS account, we set default quotas on these resources on a per-Region basis [Source: aws/EC2/Amazon EC2 service quotas] 4. Instance type limits: There is a limit on the total number of instances that you can launch in a Region, and there are additional limits on some instance types [Source: aws/EC2/Instance types > Instance limits] 5. Fleet quotas: The usual Amazon EC2 quotas apply to instances launched by an EC2 Fleet or a Spot Fleet, including Spot Instance limits and volume limits [Source: aws/EC2/Fleet quotas] For more information about the default limits, requesting increases in your current limits, or other details, refer to the provided context.

8530d8c77ae6276b52ef928663705bb53e8a28c8.text:
AWS Lambda is a cloud computing service offered by Amazon Web Services (AWS) that allows developers to run their code without provisioning or managing servers. It executes the code in response to events and automatically manages the underlying compute resources [Source: aws/Lambda/AWS Lambda Developer Guide].

The main features of AWS Lambda, as demonstrated by the provided context, include:

1. Logging: AWS Lambda provides built-in logging capabilities for monitoring function execution.
2. Environment Variables: Users can configure environment variables to customize their functions.
3. AWS X-Ray Tracing: This feature allows users to trace requests and understand how they are being processed across distributed applications.
4. Layers: Lambda Layers allow you to reuse common code across multiple functions.
5. Unit Tests: AWS Lambda supports unit testing for function development.
6. AWS SDK: The AWS Software Development Kit (SDK) is used within the sample application to interact with other AWS services.

For a more detailed look at these features and others, see [Blank Function Sample Application for AWS Lambda](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/lambda/latest/dg/samples-blank-nodejs.html) in the developer guide [Source: aws/Lambda/Blank function (Node.js)].

a95ffce7c8e5818007f9649ed7f0bd4d1377b660.text:
Based on the provided context, AWS DynamoDB is a managed NoSQL database service offered by Amazon Web Services (AWS) [Source: aws/DynamoDB/Guidelines for contributing]. Its main features include:

1. Fully managed: DynamoDB eliminates the need to manage servers or handle scaling issues, as it automatically scales up and down based on application traffic.
2. Fast performance: It provides single-digit millisecond latency at any scale [Source: aws/DynamoDB/Guidelines for contributing].
3. Secure: DynamoDB offers encryption at rest using keys managed by AWS Key Management Service (KMS) and secure HTTPS data transfer between your application and DynamoDB [Source: aws/DynamoDB/Guidelines for contributing].
4. Flexible data model: It supports both key-value and document data models, allowing developers to store and retrieve any amount of data with ease [Source: aws/DynamoDB/Guidelines for contributing].
5. Highly available and durable: DynamoDB replicates data across multiple Availability Zones (AZs) within a Region to ensure high availability and durability [Source: aws/DynamoDB/Guidelines for contributing].
6. Backup and restore: It offers automatic backups with point-in-time recovery, allowing users to recover their data in case of accidental deletion or corruption [Source: aws/DynamoDB/Guidelines for contributing].

1f46e03b688b99edc59e2250a23f2cb998e114b5.text: Based on the provided context, AWS RDS (Relational Database Service) is a managed database service offered by Amazon Web Services. Its main features include: 1. Automated tasks such as backups, patch management, software updates, and failure recovery [Source: aws/EC2/Task 3: Manually connect your EC2 instance to your RDS database by creating security groups and assigning them to the instances] 2. Support for several database engines like MySQL, PostgreSQL, MariaDB, Oracle, and SQL Server [Source: aws/EC2/Task 2: Create an RDS database and automatically connect it to your EC2 instance] 3. The ability to create a new RDS database and automatically connect it to an EC2 instance [Source: aws/EC2/Task 2: Create an RDS database and automatically connect it to your EC2 instance] 4. The option to create a MySQL RDS database [Source: aws/EC2/Task 1: Create an RDS database – *optional*] 5. The ability to delete an RDS database using the console [Source: aws/EC2/Clean up > Delete your RDS database] 6. The service allows users to choose from various instance types and sizes based on their specific needs [Source: aws/EC2/Task 3: Manually connect your EC2 instance to your RDS database by creating security groups and assigning them to the instances > Steps to create new security groups and add them to the instances] [Source: aws/EC2/Task 2: Create an RDS database and automatically connect it to your EC2 instance > Steps to create an RDS database] [Source: aws/EC2/Task 1: Create an RDS database – *optional* > Steps to create an RDS database]

6e6d0204f1c6df95a580c23b8cb431c8f6e4fde6.text: AWS Elastic Container Service (ECS) is a cloud computing service provided by Amazon Web Services that allows users to run, manage, and scale containerized applications in the AWS cloud [Source: aws/ECS/Account settings]. The main features of AWS ECS include: 1. Opting in or out of specific account settings for each AWS Region at the account level or for a specific user or role [Source: aws/ECS/Account settings] 2. Using Amazon Managed Policies such as `AmazonECS_FullAccess` to grant administrative access to ECS resources and access to the AWS services that ECS is integrated with for all of its features [Source: aws/ECS/AWS managed policies for Amazon Elastic Container Service > AmazonECS_FullAccess] 3. Support for Identity-based policies, Policy actions, and Principal permissions [Source: aws/ECS/How Amazon Elastic Container Service works with IAM] 4. Running tasks on Linux instances that are registered into an ECS cluster [Source: aws/ECS/Linux instances].

Based on the provided context, Azure Blob Storage is a cloud storage service offered by Microsoft Azure for storing large amounts of unstructured data, such as text or binary files. Its main capabilities include:

1. Low-cost, tiered storage with high availability and disaster recovery capabilities [Source: azure/Blob Storage/Introduction to Azure Data Lake Storage]
2. File system semantics, file-level security, and scale [Source: azure/Blob Storage/Introduction to Azure Data Lake Storage]
3. Enabling hierarchical namespace and unlocking capabilities such as file and directory-level security, faster operations, and big data analytics workloads with the upgrade to Azure Data Lake Storage Gen2 [Source: azure/Blob Storage/Upgrade Azure Blob Storage with Azure Data Lake Storage capabilities]

c4f50f327b118f122373316dbf6211659514e9b0.text: Based on the available documentation, Azure Storage supports Microsoft Entra ID in all public regions and national clouds [Source: azure/Blob Storage/Authorize access to blobs using Microsoft Entra ID].

081417c8999263f31460539ce5f2d98a79c8bdb8.text: Based on the provided documentation, Azure Entra ID (also known as Microsoft Entra ID) is an authentication method for authorizing access to Azure resources. Its main capabilities include authenticating applications with Microsoft Entra ID credentials [Source: azure/Blob Storage/Use Java to manage directories and files in Azure Data Lake Storage > Authorize access and connect to data resources > [Microsoft Entra ID](#tab/azure-ad)]. It is recommended for managing directories, files, ACLs, listing blobs, downloading blobs, and listing blob containers in Azure Blob Storage [Sources: azure/Blob Storage/Use Java to manage directories and files in Azure Data Lake Storage > Authorize access and connect to data resources > [Microsoft Entra ID](#tab/azure-ad), azure/Blob Storage/List blobs with Java > Set up your environment > Authorization, azure/Blob Storage/Download a blob with Java > Set up your environment > Authorization, azure/Blob Storage/List blob containers with Java > Set up your environment > Authorization]. To use Azure Entra ID, you need to assign an appropriate Azure role-based access control (Azure RBAC) role to your security principal [Source: azure/Blob Storage/Use Java to manage ACLs in Azure Data Lake Storage > Connect to the account > [Microsoft Entra ID (recommended)](#tab/entra-id)].

fd2dc579e7fd0820def5eac22be56b93dd4ea9ba.text:
Azure Virtual Network is a cloud service that provides a virtual network infrastructure as an isolated section of the Azure global network [Source: azure/Virtual Network/Azure virtual network traffic routing > System routes > Optional default routes]. Its main capabilities include:

1. Routing traffic within the virtual network and to other Azure services, with the ability to add custom routes for specific address prefixes [Source: azure/Virtual Network/Azure virtual network traffic routing > System routes > Optional default routes].
2. Enhanced connection setup performance for certain virtual machine types (Dlnv6, Dnsv6, and Ensv6) [Source: azure/Virtual Network/Network optimized virtual machine connection acceleration (Preview) > Supported virtual machine size families].
3. Integrating with Kubernetes Pods and Docker containers through the Azure Virtual Network container network interface (CNI) plug-in, which provides capabilities such as assigning IP addresses from a virtual network to containers, connecting them directly to other containers and virtual network resources, and applying network security groups and routes [Source: azure/Virtual Network/Enable containers to use Azure Virtual Network capabilities].
4. Support for both IPv4 and IPv6-capable Internet clients [Source: azure/Virtual Network/What is IPv6 for Azure Virtual Network? > Capabilities].

b3b59338c4b6f405e33583174ba790f399030816.text:
Based on the provided context, the service limits for Azure Functions can be found in two different sections: [Azure Functions hosting options > Service limits] and [Azure Functions hosting options > Consumption plan limits]. Here's a summary of some key limits:

1. [Source: azure/Azure Functions/Azure Functions hosting options > Service limits]:
   - Maximum number of functions in a function app: 20,000
   - Maximum number of triggers per function app: 500
   - Maximum number of managed dependencies per function app: 1000
   - Maximum size of a function app package: 3 GB
   - Maximum execution time for a function: 10 minutes
   - Maximum outgoing data transfer per region per month: 2 GB
   - Maximum incoming data transfer per region per month: 5 GB

2. [Source: azure/Azure Functions/Azure Functions hosting options > Consumption plan limits]:
   - Maximum number of instances per function app: 10,000
   - Maximum memory per instance: 2.8 GB
   - Maximum execution time for a function: 5 minutes (for consumption plan)
   - Maximum outgoing data transfer per function app per month: 32 GB
   - Maximum incoming data transfer per function app per month: 10 GB

Based on the provided context, Azure Virtual Machines (VMs) are a service offered by Azure [Source: azure/Virtual Network/Network optimized virtual machine connection acceleration (Preview) > Supported virtual machine size families]. The main capabilities of Azure Virtual Machines include:

1. Running various operating systems and applications [Source: azure/Virtual Network/Enable containers to use Azure Virtual Network capabilities]
2. Utilizing enhanced connection setup capabilities by using supported virtual machine types such as Dlnv6, Dnsv6, and Ensv6 [Source: azure/Virtual Network/Network optimized virtual machine connection acceleration (Preview) > Supported virtual machine size families]
3. Providing network performance tuning options like configuring TCP TIME_WAIT settings and source port range [Source: azure/Virtual Network/TCP/IP performance tuning for Azure VMs > Virtual network factors that can affect performance > VM maximum outbound throughput]
4. Supporting both Linux and Windows platforms [Source: azure/Virtual Network/Enable containers to use Azure Virtual Network capabilities]
5. Allowing the assignment of virtual network IP addresses, connection to peered virtual networks, access to services with pods, application of network security groups and routes directly to pods, placement behind an Azure internal or public load balancer, assigning pods a public IP address, and using pods to work seamlessly with Kubernetes resources [Source: azure/Virtual Network/Enable containers to use Azure Virtual Network capabilities]
6. Encrypting traffic between Azure Virtual Machines by creating a DTLS tunnel [Source: azure/Virtual Network/What is Azure Virtual Network encryption?].

Azure AKS, or Azure Kubernetes Service, is a managed container orchestration service provided by Microsoft Azure. Its main capabilities include:

1. Deploying, scaling, and managing Kubernetes clusters in Azure without the need to set up and maintain infrastructure [Source: azure/Virtual Network/Deploy the Azure Virtual Network container network interface plug-in].
2. Integrating with Azure Virtual Network to provide virtual network capabilities to Kubernetes Pods and Docker containers [Source: azure/Virtual Network/Deploy the Azure Virtual Network container network interface plug-in].
3. Allowing deployment of serverless functions using the Azure Functions on Kubernetes with KEDA feature [Source: azure/Azure Functions/Azure Functions on Kubernetes with KEDA > Managing KEDA and functions in Kubernetes].
4. Utilizing advanced networking to deploy a Kubernetes cluster in an existing or new virtual network [Source: azure/Virtual Network/Enable containers to use Azure Virtual Network capabilities > Use the plug-in].
5. Supporting up to 16,000 pods in a virtual network, with limits different from regular virtual machines [Source: azure/Virtual Network/Enable containers to use Azure Virtual Network capabilities > Limits].

a8a73c38d1ecb290ad4e2daaff26431f84f7aeb6.text:
Based on the provided context, the Amazon EKS User Guide does not contain information about setting up AWS EKS for a production workload directly. However, it does provide details about using AsciiDoc for editing and contributing to the documentation source files, which can be used as a starting point for understanding the process [Source: aws/EKS/Amazon EKS User Guide].

To set up AWS EKS for a production workload, you should refer to the official AWS documentation on creating and deploying Kubernetes applications on Amazon EKS. Here's an outline of the steps involved:

1. Prerequisites: Set up necessary AWS services like IAM roles, VPC, and ECR repository [Source: aws/EKS/Amazon EKS User Guide]
2. Install AWS CLI and kubectl [Source: aws/EKS/Amazon EKS User Guide]
3. Create an Amazon EKS cluster [Source: aws/EKS/Creating a Cluster]
4. Deploy applications to the created cluster using Helm or Kubernetes manifests [Source: aws/EKS/Deploying Applications]
5. Configure Ingress and load balancing for your applications [Source: aws/EKS/Ingress Controllers]
6. Monitor and manage your EKS cluster using AWS CloudWatch, Amazon CloudTrail, and other tools [Source: aws/EKS/Monitoring and Troubleshooting]

For more detailed information, you can refer to the official AWS documentation on [Creating a Cluster](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/eks/latest/userguide/create-cluster.html), [Deploying Applications](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/eks/latest/userguide/deploy-application.html), and [Ingress Controllers](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/eks/latest/userguide/ingress-controllers.html).

f44852f9b482d4bbfd588e203e3b7a119460ef42.text: In a production environment, you should configure your RDS database and EC2 instance to meet your specific needs. Here's how you can set up AWS RDS for a production workload based on the provided context: 1. Create an RDS database: - Engine type: MySQL [Source: aws/EC2/Task 1: Create an RDS database – *optional* > Steps to create an RDS database] - DB instance identifier: **[Your custom identifier]** - DB instance class: Choose a suitable instance class based on your workload requirements [Source: aws/EC2/Task 1: Create an RDS database – *optional* > Steps to create an RDS database] - Configure other settings as per your specific needs, such as storage type, storage size, and backup options. 2. Create a security group for the RDS database: - If you're using the automatic connection feature, the security groups will be created automatically [Source: aws/EC2/Tutorial: Connect an Amazon EC2 instance to an Amazon RDS database > Considerations] - If you're not using the automatic connection feature, you'll need to manually create and assign the security group [Source: aws/EC2/Tutorial: Connect an Amazon EC2 instance to an Amazon RDS database > Considerations] 3. Create an EC2 instance: - Launch the EC2 instance in a suitable VPC and subnet [Source: aws/EC2/Tutorial: Connect an Amazon EC2 instance to an Amazon RDS database > Considerations] - Choose a suitable instance type based on your workload requirements [Source: aws/EC2/Task 1: Create an RDS database – *optional* > Steps to create an RDS database] 4. Connect the EC2 instance to the RDS database: - If you're using the automatic connection feature, the connection will be configured automatically [Source: aws/EC2/Tutorial: Connect an Amazon EC2 instance to an Amazon RDS database > Steps to connect an EC2 instance to an RDS database] - If you're not using the automatic connection feature, you'll need to manually configure the security groups and network settings [Source: aws/EC2/Tutorial: Connect an Amazon EC2 instance to an Amazon RDS database > Considerations]

fa34299c4ae743ba43ccee1df1001b0159ae6ef1.text: To set up an Amazon Virtual Private Cloud (VPC) for a production workload in AWS, follow these steps as described in [Source: aws/EC2/Infrastructure security in Amazon EC2 > Network isolation]: 1. Create a VPC by specifying a unique IP address range and configure it according to your network requirements. [Source: aws/EC2/Infrastructure security in Amazon EC2 > Network isolation] 2. Create subnets within the VPC, isolating different tiers of your application (e.g., web, application, and database) or organizational entities. Use private subnets for instances that should not be accessed directly from the internet. [Source: aws/EC2/Infrastructure security in Amazon EC2 > Network isolation] 3. To call the Amazon EC2 API from your VPC using private IP addresses, use AWS PrivateLink. [Source: aws/EC2/Infrastructure security in Amazon EC2 > Network isolation] 4. Ensure that the EC2 instance and your RDS database are located in the same Region to use the automatic connection feature. [Source: aws/EC2/Tutorial: Connect an Amazon EC2 instance to an Amazon RDS database > Considerations] 5. The connectivity between the EC2 instance and the RDS database is configured by two security groups—one for your EC2 instance, and one for your RDS database. If you use the automatic connection feature in the EC2 console or RDS console to configure the connectivity, the security groups are automatically created and assigned to the EC2 instance and RDS database. [Source: aws/EC2/Tutorial: Connect an Amazon EC2 instance to an Amazon RDS database > Considerations] 6. Be flexible about which instance types you request and in which Availability Zones you can deploy your workload to give Spot a better chance to find and allocate your required amount of compute capacity. [Source: aws/EC2/Best practices for EC2 Spot > Be flexible about instance types and Availability Zones]

f2d21adc14d4a7497bcf1f1af94baeab83876617.text: To deploy a containerized application on AWS ECS, you can use the AWS Cloud Development Kit (AWS CDK) as described in the context [1]. Here are the steps: 1. Write an app using one of the supported languages by the AWS CDK and define one or more stacks. 2. Use the `aws-ecs-patterns` module to model resources for Amazon ECS, such as the `ApplicationLoadBalancedFargateService` construct which deploys an Amazon ECS service on Fargate behind an application load balancer. 3. Save the source file and run the `cdk synth` command in your application's main directory to synthesize an AWS CloudFormation template from it. 4. Run the `cdk deploy` command in your application's main directory to deploy the service in your AWS account, after approving the IAM policies generated by the AWS CDK. [Source: aws/ECS/Getting started with Amazon ECS using the AWS CDK]

3184cf2cbf2e9a5aa5eab290b933a075e4f67e08.text: To set up AWS ECS for a production workload, follow these steps based on the provided documentation: 1. Create an Amazon ECS service that uses the blue/green deployment type by following the instructions in [Creating an Amazon ECS service in the classic console](create-service.md) [Source: aws/ECS/Blue/Green deployment with CodeDeploy > Amazon ECS console experience]. 2. Configure the service to use either an Application Load Balancer or Network Load Balancer [Source: aws/ECS/Blue/Green deployment with CodeDeploy > Blue/Green Deployment Considerations]. - Add a production listener to the load balancer, which is used to route production traffic. - An optional test listener can be added to the load balancer, which is used to route test traffic during a deployment. - Both the production and test listeners must belong to the same load balancer. - Define a target group for the load balancer that routes traffic to the original task set in a service through the production listener. 3. When creating a CodeDeploy deployment for an Amazon ECS service, CodeDeploy creates a replacement task set (or green task set) in the deployment [Source: aws/ECS/Blue/Green deployment with CodeDeploy > Blue/Green Deployment Considerations]. If you added a test listener to the load balancer, CodeDeploy routes your test traffic to the replacement task set. This is when you can run any validation tests. Then CodeDeploy reroutes the production traffic from the original task set to the replacement task set according to the traffic rerouting settings for the deployment group. 4. To invoke a Lambda function after the production traffic has been rerouted to the replacement task set, you can use the **AfterAllowTraffic** lifecycle event hook [Source: aws/ECS/Updating a service using the classic console] or [aws/ECS/Updating a service using the classic console]. For more information about lifecycle hooks, see [AppSpec 'hooks' Section](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/codedeploy/latest/userguide/reference-appspec-file-structure-hooks.html) in the *AWS CodeDeploy User Guide*.

1298e18792a11f2fcfc4b41cda6492051a0d1f91.text:
To secure an Amazon Virtual Private Cloud (VPC) using IAM policies, you can create a role and policy that grants users permissions to enable or disable a VPC for ClassicLink, link or unlink an instance to a ClassicLink-enabled VPC, and view ClassicLink-enabled VPCs and linked EC2-Classic instances [Source: aws/EC2/ClassicLink]. Additionally, you can create IAM policies that restrict access to specific regions, control the use of resources like instances, volumes, snapshots, and launch templates, and allow a specific instance to view resources in other AWS services [Source: aws/EC2/Example policies for working with the AWS CLI or an AWS SDK].

When managing access using policies, you can use resource-based policies that are inline policies located within the service. However, you cannot use AWS managed policies from IAM in a resource-based policy [Source: aws/ECS/Identity and Access Management for Amazon Elastic Container Service].

To further secure your VPC, follow these guidelines and recommendations when creating or editing identity-based policies:
1. Use AWS managed policies that grant permissions for common use cases to get started, then reduce permissions further by defining AWS customer managed policies specific to your use cases [Source: aws/ECS/Identity-based policy examples for Amazon Elastic Container Service].
2. Apply least-privilege permissions by granting only the permissions required to perform a task [Source: aws/ECS/Identity-based policy examples for Amazon Elastic Container Service].
3. Use conditions in IAM policies to limit access to actions and resources [Source: aws/ECS/Identity-based policy examples for Amazon Elastic Container Service].
4. Use IAM Access Analyzer to validate your IAM policies to ensure secure and functional permissions [Source: aws/ECS/Identity-based policy examples for Amazon Elastic Container Service].
5. Require multi-factor authentication (MFA) when API operations are called by adding MFA conditions to your policies [Source: aws/ECS/Identity-based policy examples for Amazon Elastic Container Service].

54f2183e2738cd981eaf339d1644d61e72018f69.text: To monitor AWS Lambda functions with CloudWatch, you can follow these steps as described in the context: 1. First, create a CloudWatch Events rule for your Lambda function [Source: aws/ECS/Tutorial: Listening for Amazon ECS CloudWatch Events > Step 2: Register an event rule]. - Open the CloudWatch console at [https://console.aws.amazon.com/cloudwatch/](https://console.aws.amazon.com/cloudwatch/) - Choose **Events**, **Rules**, **Create rule** - For **Event Source**, choose **Lambda function** as the event source - Select your Lambda function - Choose **Configure details** - For **Rule definition**, type a name and description for your rule and choose **Create rule** 2. Alternatively, you can use AWS Lambda and Amazon CloudWatch Events to handle event notifications programmatically for other AWS services like EC2 [Source: aws/EC2/Monitor your policies using CloudWatch Events]. After the metrics are visible in the CloudWatch console, create CloudWatch alarms on the desired metrics. For example, to create an alarm that triggers when the cluster's memory reservation is above 75%, follow the steps outlined in [To create a CloudWatch alarm on a metric](#answer_1) [Source: aws/ECS/Tutorial: Scaling container instances with CloudWatch alarms > Step 1: Create a CloudWatch alarm for a metric] 3. You can also set up AWS Lambda functions to listen for Amazon ECS task events and write them out to a CloudWatch Logs log stream, as described in [AWS > ECS > Tutorial: Listening for Amazon ECS CloudWatch Events] [Source: aws/ECS/Tutorial: Listening for Amazon ECS CloudWatch Events].

[Azure Functions](#3) - Identity management for Virtual Networks involves controlling access to network resources and ensuring that only authorized users and services can modify network configurations. Proper identity controls prevent unauthorized network changes and maintain network security posture.

- Use Azure RBAC for network resource access: Assign appropriate built-in roles such as Network Contributor or custom roles with specific permissions to control who can create, modify, or delete virtual networks and related resources. Follow the principle of least privilege. [Source: azure/Virtual Network/Secure your Virtual Network deployment > Identity management]

- Enable Microsoft Entra ID integration with SSO: Use Microsoft Entra ID as the centralized identity provider for managing access to network resources and related Azure services. Implement single sign-on (SSO) rather than configuring individual standalone credentials per service to reduce attack surface and minimize password requirements. [Source: azure/Virtual Network/Secure your Virtual Network deployment > Identity management]

2. [Azure Functions](#4) - To connect an MCP server on Azure Functions to a Foundry Agent Service agent, you can use custom OAuth with a Microsoft Entra app registration. This involves exposing an API and getting the required credentials from the Azure portal. [Source: azure/Azure Functions/Connect an MCP server on Azure Functions to a Foundry Agent Service agent > Get credentials > [OAuth identity](#tab/oauth-id)]

For more information about Azure Virtual Networks and availability zones, you can refer to [What is Azure Virtual Network?](azure/Virtual Network/What is Azure Virtual Network? > Virtual networks and availability zones) [Source: azure/Virtual Network/What is Azure Virtual Network? > Virtual networks and availability zones].

61814424038d1dd815f43748ba86643f8f83953b.text:
To configure networking for Azure Cosmos DB, you can deploy your function app in an App Service plan that provides virtual network integration with the virtual network where your Cosmos DB resource is secured [Source: AZURE > Azure Functions > Azure Functions networking options > Connecting to Azure Services through a virtual network > Virtual network triggers (non-HTTP) > App Service plan and App Service Environment with virtual network triggers].

Additionally, you can create an output binding for Azure Cosmos DB in your function app. Here's how to do it:

1. In the Azure portal, navigate to and select the function app you created previously.
2. Select **Functions**, and then select the HttpTrigger function.
3. Select **Integration** and **+ Add output**.
4. Use the **Create Output** settings as specified in the table:

   | Setting      | Suggested value  | Description                                |
   | ------------ | ---------------- | ------------------------------------------ |
   | **Binding Type** | Azure Cosmos DB | Name of the binding type to select to create the output binding to Azure Cosmos DB. |
   | **Document parameter name** | taskDocument | Name that refers to the Azure Cosmos DB object in code. |
   | **Database name** | taskDatabase | Name of database to save documents. |
   | **Collection name** | taskCollection | Name of the database collection. |
   | **If true, creates the Azure Cosmos DB database and collection** | Yes | The collection doesn't already exist, so create it. |
   | **Azure Cosmos DB account connection** | New setting | Select **New**, then choose **Azure Cosmos DB Account** and the **Database account** you created earlier, and then select **OK**. Creates an application setting for your account connection. This setting is used by the binding to connect to the database. |

5. Select **OK** to create the binding [Source: azure/Azure Functions/Store unstructured data using Azure Functions and Azure Cosmos DB > Add an output binding].

To implement backup and disaster recovery for Azure Virtual Machines, you can utilize several options as described in the documentation [Source: azure/Virtual Machines/Introduction to Azure managed disks > Backup and disaster recovery options]. These include:

1. Built-in redundancy options like locally redundant storage (LRS) and zone-redundant storage (ZRS).
2. Azure Backup, which is available through Azure Disk Backup.
3. Managed disk snapshots.
4. Azure Site Recovery.

For more detailed information on each option, see [Backup and disaster recovery for Azure managed disks](https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/virtual-machines/disks-manage#backup-and-disaster-recovery).

To create managed disk snapshots, follow the instructions in [Create a snapshot of a virtual hard disk](https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/virtual-machines/windows/snapshot-copy-managed-disk#create-a-snapshot-of-a-virtual-hard-disk).

For backup and recovery of Virtual Networks, refer to [Backup and recovery for Virtual Networks](https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/virtual-network/security-best-practices#backup-and-recovery) which includes exporting network configurations, documenting network architecture, testing recovery procedures, backing up connected resources and customer-managed keys, preparing redundant networking infrastructure, and more.

To learn how to create managed disk snapshots, see [Create a snapshot of a virtual hard disk] (Source: azure/Virtual Machines/Introduction to Azure managed disks > Backup and disaster recovery options > Snapshots).

To implement backup and disaster recovery for Azure Functions, you can use Durable Functions' built-in HTTP APIs or client functions that are triggered via HTTP. All state in Durable Functions is persisted in Azure Storage by default. A task hub is a logical container for Azure Storage resources used for orchestrations and entities.

In case of disaster recovery, an active/passive configuration can be deployed where a backup (passive) function app is deployed to a different region. [Azure Traffic Manager](https://azure.microsoft.com/services/traffic-manager/) monitors the primary (active) function app for HTTP availability and fails over to the backup function app when the primary app fails.

[Source: azure/Azure Functions/Disaster recovery and geo-distribution in Durable Functions > Background]

To configure IAM permissions for Google Cloud VPC, follow these steps as described in the provided context:

1. Set up Direct VPC egress for 2nd gen Cloud Functions by granting the Cloud Run Invoker (roles/run.invoker) role to your function's service account [Source: gcp/Cloud Functions/Configure Direct VPC egress for 2nd gen functions > Set up IAM permissions]

   - Ensure that Cloud Run has access to the VPC network by using one of the following methods:
     a) Use the default Cloud Run Service Agent role (roles/run.serviceAgent) which contains the necessary permissions.
     b) Grant the Cloud Run service agent with additional permissions on the project: compute.networks.get, compute.subnetworks.get, compute.subnetworks.use, compute.addresses.list, compute.addresses.create (for dual-stack subnets with external IPv6), and compute.addresses.delete (for dual-stack subnets with external IPv6) [Source: gcp/Cloud Functions/Configure Direct VPC egress for 2nd gen functions > Set up IAM permissions]
     c) Grant the Compute Network User role (roles/compute.networkUser) on the Cloud Run Service Agent service account, and if you have subnets with external IPv6, also grant the Compute Public IP Admin role (roles/compute.publicIpAdmin) [Source: gcp/Cloud Functions/Configure Direct VPC egress for 2nd gen functions > Set up IAM permissions]

2. If you are using a Shared VPC, grant the Shared VPC Admin role (roles/compute.xpnAdmin) to the service account that manages the shared VPC host project [Source: gcp/Compute Engine/Compute Engine IAM roles and permissionsStay organized with collectionsSave and categorize content based on your preferences. > Predefined Compute Engine IAM roles > Compute Shared VPC Admin]

3. If you are using GKE, configure Workload Identity Federation for GKE to exchange the federated access token for an access token from the IAM Service Account Credentials API [Source: gcp/GKE/Authenticate to Google Cloud APIs from GKE workloadsStay organized with collectionsSave and categorize content based on your preferences. > Configure applications to use Workload Identity Federation for GKE > Configure authorization and principals]
   - Create a Kubernetes ServiceAccount for your application to use, grant permissions to specific Google Cloud resources that your application needs to access [Source: gcp/GKE/Authenticate to Google Cloud APIs from GKE workloadsStay organized with collectionsSave and categorize content based on your preferences. > Configure applications to use Workload Identity Federation for GKE > Configure authorization and principals]

To configure IAM permissions for Google Cloud Compute Engine, follow these steps:

1. Grant the necessary roles to a member on a specific service account [Source: gcp/Compute Engine/Compute Engine IAM roles and permissions]. The recommended role for creating an instance template and a Managed Instance Group (MIG) is the Compute Instance Admin (v1) role (roles/compute.instanceAdmin.v1).

2. To grant the Service Account User role (roles/iam.serviceAccountUser), which, when combined with the Compute Instance Admin (v1) role, allows members to create and manage instances that use a service account, you can do so in one of two ways:
   - Grant the role to a member on a specific service account.

3. For more information about granting roles, see [Manage access to projects, folders, and organizations](https://cloud.google.com/iam/docs/granting-roles) in the IAM documentation.

424ff06d9eae6208c7030f5fef58de3e93157eb3.text:
Based on the provided documentation, there are a few ways to configure IAM permissions for Google Cloud Pub/Sub.

1. To grant the necessary permissions to the Cloud Functions service account (service-PROJECT_NUMBER@gcf-admin-robot.iam.gserviceaccount.com) so that it can access Pub/Sub, you can use the following command:

```bash
accounts add-iam-policy-binding projects/-/serviceAccounts/SERVICE_ACCOUNT_NAME@PROJECT_ID.iam.gserviceaccount.com --member MEMBER --role roles/iam.serviceAccountUser' [Source: gcp/Cloud Functions/Troubleshoot Cloud Run functions > Deployment > Deployment service account missing Pub/Sub permissions when deploying an event-driven function]
```

Replace `SERVICE_ACCOUNT_NAME` with the name of your service account and `MEMBER` with a user or service account that has the necessary permissions to modify IAM roles.

2. When creating a Cloud Functions Pub/Sub event-driven function in Go, you can use the `roles/pubsub.admin` role to grant the necessary permissions:

```bash
gcloud iam service-accounts add-iam-policy-binding SERVICE_ACCOUNT --member "serviceAccount:PROJECT_ID@appspot.gserviceaccount.com" --role roles/pubsub.admin [Source: gcp/Cloud Functions/Locally test your function > Cloud Pub/Sub event-driven function example > Go]
```

Replace `SERVICE_ACCOUNT` with the name of your service account and `PROJECT_ID` with your project ID.

3. In a Terraform deployment, you can create a service account and grant it the necessary roles:

```hcl
resource "google_service_account" "default" {
  account_id   = "test-gcf-sa"
  display_name = "Test Service Account"
}

resource "google_project_iam_member" "cloudrun" {
  project = google_project.default.number
  role    = "roles/pubsub.admin"
  member  = "serviceAccount:${google_service_account.default.email}"
} [Source: gcp/Cloud Functions/Terraform Pub/Sub TutorialStay organized with collectionsSave and categorize content based on your preferences.]
```

Based on the available documentation, I cannot find sufficient information to fully answer this question about implementing auto-scaling specifically with Google Cloud VPC. However, I can provide some related information that might help you understand how auto-scaling and VPC networking work in Google Cloud.

1. Auto-scaling: In Google Cloud, you can use managed instance groups (MIGs) to create and manage a set of identical instances across multiple zones for load balancing and auto-scaling purposes. [Source: gcp/Compute Engine/Managed Instance Groups]

2. VPC Networking: When using MIGs, you can configure them to use a specific VPC network, subnet, or IP range. This allows instances to communicate securely with each other and access resources within the same VPC network. [Source: gcp/Compute Engine/Managed Instance Groups > Configuring instance groups]

3. Network Tiers: Google Cloud offers two networking tiers (Standard and Premium) that can affect your auto-scaling strategy by influencing network performance, reliability, and cost. [Source: gcp/Compute Engine/Networking overview for VMs]

4. Firewall Rules: You can also use VPC firewall rules to control traffic between instances within the same VPC network or between instances and resources outside of it. [Source: gcp/Compute Engine/Networking overview for VMs > Routes, Firewall rules]

To implement auto-scaling specifically with Google Cloud VPC, you may need to combine these concepts and configure them appropriately according to your application's requirements. For more detailed information on implementing auto-scaling in Google Cloud, I recommend checking the following resources:

1. [Auto scaling](https://cloud.google.com/compute/docs/autoscaler/)
2. [Managed Instance Groups](https://cloud.google.com/compute/docs/instance-groups)
3. [VPC Networking](https://cloud.google.com/vpc/docs/overview)

Based on the provided documentation, there is no information that directly compares or contrasts when to use AWS VPC (Virtual Private Cloud) instead of AWS Lambda. Both AWS VPC and AWS Lambda are separate services with different functionalities.

AWS Lambda is a serverless compute service that lets you run your code without provisioning or managing servers [Source: aws/Lambda/AWS Lambda Developer Guide]. On the other hand, AWS VPC enables you to launch AWS resources in a virtual network that you define [Source: aws/VPC/User Guide].

To determine when to use one over the other, it's essential to understand their purposes and how they can be integrated. For example, if you need to run code without managing servers, you might choose AWS Lambda. If you require more control over your network environment, such as configuring subnets, security groups, and networking components, then AWS VPC would be a better choice.

However, it's important to note that AWS Lambda functions can be placed within a VPC for increased security and control [Source: aws/Lambda/AWS Lambda Developer Guide]. This integration allows you to access resources within the VPC from your Lambda function while still benefiting from the serverless nature of AWS Lambda.

In summary, based on the available documentation, I cannot find sufficient information to fully answer this question directly. To determine when to use AWS VPC instead of AWS Lambda, it's essential to understand their purposes and how they can be integrated.

It's ideal for deploying, scaling, and managing containerized applications using Kubernetes. 2. **AWS EC2** [Source: aws/EC2/Linux accelerated computing instances > Instances with AWS Inferentia] is a web service that provides secure, resizable compute capacity in the cloud. It offers various instance types optimized for different workloads such as general purpose, compute-optimized, memory-optimized, storage-optimized, and accelerated computing instances (like Inf1 and Inf2 instances which are optimized for machine learning inference). In summary, AWS EKS is a managed Kubernetes service for deploying and managing containerized applications, while AWS EC2 offers scalable compute capacity for various workloads, including machine learning inference with the Inf1 and Inf2 instances. For a more comprehensive comparison, you may want to refer to the official documentation of each service on the AWS website. [Source: aws/EKS/Amazon EKS User Guide, aws/EC2/Linux accelerated computing instances > Instances with AWS Inferentia]

f2422dd6797e4ed7b6008a3f96fef1e50cbdd573.text: Based on the provided documentation, both AWS Lambda and AWS EC2 are services offered by Amazon Web Services (AWS), but they serve different purposes. 1. **AWS Lambda** [Source: aws/EC2/Create Amazon EventBridge rules to monitor EC2 Fleet events]: It is a serverless compute service that runs your code in response to events and automatically manages the underlying compute resources. Lambda functions are stateless, meaning they don't have any permanent storage. 2. **AWS EC2** [Source: aws/EC2/Create Amazon EventBridge rules to monitor EC2 Fleet events]: It is a virtual computing environment (IaaS) that lets you rent virtual computers on demand. With EC2, you have full control over the instances and can configure them as needed, including setting up storage, operating systems, and applications. In summary, while AWS Lambda allows you to run code in response to events without provisioning or managing servers, AWS EC2 provides a virtual computing environment where you can deploy and manage your own servers.

6514527956beaea3f2d61a421daefd1146926a7e.text:
Based on the provided documentation, the context does not contain sufficient information to fully compare AWS Lambda and AWS ECS for production workloads. However, I can provide some insights about each service that might help in understanding their trade-offs.

1. AWS Lambda [Source: aws/Lambda/What is AWS Lambda?](https://aws.amazon.com/lambda/)
   - Advantages:
     - **Pay-per-use**: Only pay for the compute time you consume. There is no charge when your code is not running.
     - **Scalability**: AWS automatically manages the underlying infrastructure to handle traffic spikes and scale your application as needed.
     - **Event-driven**: Lambda functions can be triggered by various events such as changes in databases, S3 object creation, or custom events from other AWS services.
   - Disadvantages:
     - **Cold start**: When a Lambda function is not already running and needs to be started, it may take some time (a few hundred milliseconds to several seconds) for the function to become available, which can impact performance for latency-sensitive applications.
     - **Limited control over infrastructure**: Since AWS manages the underlying infrastructure, you have limited control over the resources allocated to your Lambda functions.

2. AWS ECS [Source: aws/ECS/What is Amazon Elastic Container Service?](https://aws.amazon.com/ecs/)
   - Advantages:
     - **Flexibility**: ECS supports both EC2 and Fargate launch types, allowing you to choose the infrastructure that best fits your needs.
     - **Scalability**: You can easily scale your applications by adding or removing containers as needed.
     - **Integration with other AWS services**: ECS integrates well with other AWS services such as CloudWatch, CodeDeploy, and RDS, making it easy to build complex, distributed systems.
   - Disadvantages:
     - **Infrastructure management**: Unlike Lambda, you are responsible for managing the underlying infrastructure, including scaling, patching, and security.
     - **Higher cost**: Since you pay for the resources used by your containers, the cost can be higher than using Lambda for some workloads. However, ECS may still be more cost-effective for long-running applications or those with high resource requirements.

bbe3a0e14a95655c8446d11c04b2a43a12176fcd.text:
Based on the provided documentation, both AWS VPC (Virtual Private Cloud) and AWS IAM (Identity and Access Management) serve distinct purposes within the AWS ecosystem.

AWS VPC [Source: aws/Lambda/AWS Lambda Developer Guide] is a service that lets you launch AWS resources in a logically isolated virtual network that you define. It provides network functionality like a VPN, firewall, and NAT (Network Address Translation). Use cases for AWS VPC include creating secure, scalable, multi-tier architectures, setting up a private network with specific IP address ranges, and configuring route tables to control the flow of traffic in your VPC.

On the other hand, AWS IAM [Source: aws/Lambda/AWS Lambda Developer Guide] is a service that helps you securely control access to AWS resources for your users. It allows you to manage who can use which AWS resources and what actions they can perform on those resources. Use cases for AWS IAM include creating and managing users, groups, roles, and permissions; implementing least privilege principles by granting only the necessary permissions; and auditing access logs for security analysis.

In summary, while AWS VPC is primarily used for network isolation and configuration, AWS IAM is used for identity and access management within AWS services. [Source: aws/Lambda/AWS Lambda Developer Guide, aws/Lambda/License Summary]

6006a76314b39814cde852e9e9a4488d954b897c.text:
Based on the provided documentation, I cannot find sufficient information to fully answer this question as it does not compare AWS Lambda and AWS EC2 directly in terms of trade-offs for production workloads. However, I can provide some insights about each service that might help you understand their differences.

1. **AWS Lambda** [Source: aws/Lambda/Getting started with AWS Lambda](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/lambda/latest/dg/getting-started-create-function.html)
   - Advantages:
     - **Cost-effective**: Only pay for the compute time you consume. There is no charge when your code is not running.
     - **Scalability**: AWS Lambda automatically manages the underlying infrastructure required to run your code, allowing it to scale up or down based on the workload.
     - **Event-driven**: Lambda functions can be triggered by various events such as changes in an Amazon S3 bucket, updates in DynamoDB tables, or custom events from other AWS services.
   - Disadvantages:
     - **Cold start**: When a Lambda function is not already running and needs to be invoked, there might be a delay due to the time it takes to initialize the container, which can impact performance for latency-sensitive applications.
     - **Limited control over resources**: Since AWS manages the underlying infrastructure, you have limited control over the resources allocated to your function, which may not always meet the requirements of high-performance workloads.

2. **AWS EC2** [Source: aws/EC2/Getting started with Amazon Elastic Compute Cloud](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AWSEC2/latest/UserGuide/get-set-up-for-amazon-ec2.html)
   - Advantages:
     - **Full control**: EC2 instances provide complete control over the underlying infrastructure, allowing you to customize and optimize resources for your specific workload requirements.
     - **Flexibility**: Choose from a wide variety of instance types with different CPU, memory, storage, and networking configurations to meet the needs of your application.
   - Disadvantages:
     - **Cost**: With EC2, you pay for the instances even when they are idle, which can lead to higher costs compared to Lambda if not managed properly.
     - **Scalability**: Scaling EC2 instances requires manual intervention or the use of additional services like Auto Scaling groups and Elastic Load Balancers, which may add complexity to your infrastructure management.

[AWS > EKS > Amazon EKS User Guide] On the other hand, AWS EKS is a managed Kubernetes service that makes it easy for you to run Kubernetes on AWS without needing to worry about the underlying infrastructure [Source: aws/EKS/Amazon EKS User Guide]. The docs now use AsciiDoc, an intuitive yet powerful authoring language similar to Markdown that offers advanced formatting capabilities, robust cross-referencing, enhanced security controls, and enterprise-grade documentation features. Additionally, the contribution process has been streamlined with direct editing of documentation source files, faster pull request processing, increased automation, and reduced manual steps [Source: aws/EKS/Amazon EKS User Guide]. In summary, AWS EC2 is a compute service that provides instances for various workloads, including machine learning inference. AWS EKS, on the other hand, is a managed Kubernetes service for running containerized applications on AWS. The use cases and features of these services are different, but they both offer benefits within their respective domains.

47b4788f2e7e5e43a82f1277f9c61df337a74635.text: Based on the provided context, the AWS RDS (Relational Database Service) and AWS VPC (Virtual Private Cloud) are separate services with different functionalities. 1. AWS RDS [Source: aws/EC2/Task 1: Create an RDS database – *optional*] is a managed database service that makes it easier to set up, operate, and scale a relational database in the cloud. It supports several database engine types such as MySQL, PostgreSQL, MariaDB, Oracle, and SQL Server. 2. AWS VPC [Source: aws/EC2/Migrate from EC2-Classic to a VPC], on the other hand, is a virtual network dedicated to your AWS resources. It provides you with complete control over your virtual networking environment including selection of your own IP address range, creation of subnets, and configuration of route tables and network gateways. 3. When using RDS, it can be deployed within a VPC [Source: aws/EC2/Migrate from EC2-Classic to a VPC], but they serve different purposes. An RDS instance is a database, while a VPC is a network environment for your AWS resources. 4. For automatic connection between an EC2 instance and an RDS database, both need to be in the same VPC [Source: aws/EC2/Connect your EC2 instance to an AWS resource > Automatically connect an EC2 instance to an RDS database > Automatically connect an instance and a database]. 5. When manually configuring the connection between EC2 instances and RDS databases, you may need to create and assign security groups if not using the automatic connection feature [Source: aws/EC2/Tutorial: Connect an Amazon EC2 instance to an Amazon RDS database > Considerations].

39d8bb9a05342fb9068d20cb14bda628a3859b9e.text: Based on the provided documentation, both AWS EC2 and AWS CloudWatch have distinct roles and offer different features that can be used together to monitor and manage resources. **AWS EC2 (Amazon Elastic Compute Cloud)**: - Provides scalable computing capacity in the cloud [Source: aws/EC2/Amazon Elastic Block Store \(Amazon EBS\) > Features of Amazon EBS] - Offers various instance types, including General Purpose SSD, Provisioned IOPS SSD, Throughput Optimized HDD, and Cold HDD [Source: aws/EC2/Amazon Elastic Block Store \(Amazon EBS\) > Features of Amazon EBS] - Allows creating encrypted volumes for data-at-rest encryption [Source: aws/EC2/Amazon Elastic Block Store \(Amazon EBS\) > Features of Amazon EBS] - Provides point-in-time snapshots for long-term durability and creating new volumes [Source: aws/EC2/Amazon Elastic Block Store \(Amazon EBS\) > Features of Amazon EBS] - Offers performance metrics for monitoring the performance of instances [Source: aws/EC2/Amazon Elastic Block Store \(Amazon EBS\) > Features of Amazon EBS] **AWS CloudWatch**: - A monitoring service for AWS resources and custom applications [Source: aws/CloudWatch/WhatIsCloudWatch.md] - Provides a way to collect and track metrics, monitor log files, set alarms, and automatically react to changes in your AWS resources [Source: aws/CloudWatch/WhatIsCloudWatch.md] - Offers features like Anomaly Detection Alarm for monitoring resource usage and triggering alerts based on anomalies [Source: aws/EC2/Monitor your policies using Amazon CloudWatch > Example use cases] - Allows monitoring the lifecycle of snapshots and AMIs, as well as using AWS CloudTrail for monitoring [Source: aws/EC2/Monitor the lifecycle of snapshots and AMIs] - Integrates with service quotas to alert when usage approaches a service quota [Source: aws/EC2/List the available CloudWatch metrics for your instances > Amazon EC2 usage metrics] In summary, AWS EC2 provides scalable computing capacity and various instance types, while AWS CloudWatch is a monitoring service for AWS resources and custom applications that offers features like Anomaly Detection Alarm, monitoring the lifecycle of snapshots and AMIs, and integration with service quotas. Both services can be used together to monitor and manage resources effectively.

29e47a8ee37d10cadb1b827a8959788afd835c39.text: Based on the provided documentation, the differences between AWS EKS (Elastic Kubernetes Service) and AWS ECS (Elastic Container Service) can be summarized as follows: 1. Orchestration System: AWS ECS uses its own orchestrator called Amazon ECS, while AWS EKS is built around Kubernetes as the orchestration system. [Source: aws/ECS/Amazon ECS User Guide; Source: aws/EKS/Amazon EKS User Guide] 2. Service Quotas Management: AWS ECS has integrated with AWS Service Quotas for managing service quotas, while AWS EKS does not explicitly mention such integration. [Source: aws/ECS/Amazon ECS service quotas > Managing your Amazon ECS and AWS Fargate service quotas in the AWS Management Console] 3. Documentation: AWS EKS documentation is now using AsciiDoc, an intuitive yet powerful authoring language, for direct editing of documentation source files. This feature is not explicitly mentioned for AWS ECS. [Source: aws/EKS/Amazon EKS User Guide] 4. Licensing: The documentation for both AWS ECS and AWS EKS is made available under the Creative Commons Attribution-ShareAlike 4.0 International License, but sample code within this documentation has a modified MIT license for AWS EKS. [Source: aws/EKS/License Summary; Source: aws/ECS/License Summary]

aed57f95acc6d7d0e3518d242b3836f17cf6cf7b.text: Based on the provided documentation, both AWS VPC (Virtual Private Cloud) and AWS ECS (Elastic Container Service) have distinct use cases and features. AWS VPC is a service that lets you launch AWS resources in a logically isolated virtual network that you define. It provides network-level isolation between your resources, offering administrative control over your networking environment including selection of your own IP address range, creation of subnets, and configuration of route tables and network gateways. [Source: aws/ECS/Set up to use Amazon ECS > Create a security group] On the other hand, AWS ECS is a container management service that makes it easy for developers to deploy applications on any number of Amazon Web Services (AWS) and isolate them from each other and the underlying infrastructure. It allows you to run containers on a managed cluster or on EC2 instances that you provision yourself. [Source: aws/ECS/Set up to use Amazon ECS] In terms of use cases, AWS VPC is primarily used for network isolation and configuration management, while AWS ECS is used for container orchestration and deployment of applications. However, they can be used together to create a secure environment for running containerized applications on AWS. For example, you might use AWS VPC to set up security groups that control the inbound and outbound traffic for your ECS tasks. [Source: aws/ECS/Set up to use Amazon ECS > Create a security group] For more detailed information about the features and use cases of AWS VPC and AWS ECS, you can refer to their respective documentation: - AWS VPC: [Amazon Virtual Private Cloud (VPC) User Guide](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/vpc/latest/userguide/) - AWS ECS: [AWS Elastic Container Service (ECS) User Guide](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonECS/latest/developerguide/)

a0fbd6f7e21f2301809042d4edb8b21892ad1b6e.text: Based on the provided context, AWS EKS (Amazon Elastic Kubernetes Service) and AWS IAM (Identity and Access Management) serve different purposes within the AWS ecosystem. 1. **AWS EKS**: It is a managed service that makes it easier for you to run Kubernetes on AWS without needing to set up or maintain your own cluster. The main focus of AWS EKS is on managing Kubernetes clusters, deploying applications, and providing documentation for these tasks [Source: aws/EKS/Amazon EKS User Guide]. 2. **AWS IAM**: It is a service that helps you securely control access to AWS services and resources for your users. With IAM, you can manage who has access to the AWS resources in your account and what actions they can perform [Source: aws/IAM/Overview]. In terms of use cases, AWS EKS is primarily used for managing Kubernetes clusters and deploying applications on those clusters, while AWS IAM is used for managing access control to AWS services and resources. Regarding features, AWS EKS offers AsciiDoc-Powered Documentation, a Streamlined Contribution Process, Advanced formatting capabilities, Robust cross-referencing, Enhanced security controls, and Enterprise-grade documentation features [Source: aws/EKS/Amazon EKS User Guide]. On the other hand, AWS IAM provides features such as Identity Policies, Access Policies, Role-Based Access Control (RBAC), Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA), and more for managing access control to AWS services and resources [Source: aws/IAM/Overview]. There is no direct comparison between AWS EKS and AWS IAM as they serve different purposes within the AWS ecosystem. However, when using AWS EKS, you might need to manage IAM roles for tasks execution, especially when dealing with private repositories or sensitive data [Source: aws/ECS/Amazon ECS task execution IAM role].
