154624775ed119749b791f419590691f8100e618.text: To configure Auto Scaling in AWS EC2, follow these steps: 1. First, create a launch template that specifies the Amazon EC2 instance configuration, including the AMI, instance type, key pair, security groups, and other parameters used to launch instances. You can do this by using the AWS CLI with the `create-launch-template` command [Source: aws/EC2/Launch instances from a launch template]. Here's an example of creating a launch template using the AWS CLI: ``` aws autoscaling create-launch-template --launch-template-name myTemplate --image-id ami-0abcdef1234567890 --instance-type t2.micro --key-name myKeyPair --security-group-names mySecurityGroup ``` 2. After creating the launch template, you can create or update an Amazon EC2 Auto Scaling group using this launch template. Use the `create-auto-scaling-group` or `update-auto-scaling-group` command and specify the `--launch-template` parameter [Source: aws/EC2/Launch instances from a launch template]. Here's an example of creating an Auto Scaling group using the AWS CLI: ``` aws autoscaling create-auto-scaling-group --auto-scaling-group-name myASG --launch-template-name myTemplate --min-size 2 --max-size 4 ``` 3. If you prefer to use the AWS Management Console, you can configure Service Auto Scaling for your Amazon ECS service to adjust its desired count of tasks in response to CloudWatch alarms [Source: aws/ECS/Step 5: Configuring your service to use Service Auto Scaling]. To do this, follow these steps: 1. Update your service using the classic console and choose **Configure Service Auto Scaling** [Source: aws/ECS/Updating a service using the classic console, aws/ECS/Updating a service using the classic console]. 2. Proceed to [Step 5: Configuring your service to use Service Auto Scaling](service-configure-auto-scaling.md) and complete the steps provided in that section [Source: aws/ECS/Updating a service using the classic console, aws/ECS/Updating a service using the classic console]. 3. After configuring Service Auto Scaling, choose **Update Service** to finish and update your service [Source: aws/ECS/Updating a service using the classic console, aws/ECS/Updating a service using the classic console]. 4. When creating an Auto Scaling group for a capacity provider, consider the following: - If managed termination protection is enabled when you create a capacity provider, the Auto Scaling group and each Amazon EC2 instance in the Auto Scaling group must have instance protection from scale in enabled as well [Source: aws/ECS/Creating an Auto Scaling group]. - If managed scaling is enabled when you create a capacity provider, the Auto Scaling group desired count can be set to `0` [Source: aws/ECS/Creating an Auto Scaling group]. - The Auto Scaling group can't have instance weighting settings, as instance weighting isn't supported when used with an Amazon ECS capacity provider [Source: aws/ECS/Creating an Auto Scaling group]. For more information on launch templates, creating Auto Scaling groups, and Service Auto Scaling, refer to the provided AWS documentation links.

21bd65e6b94fc72069bd2776af8926b3a89c71e5.text: Based on the provided context, there isn't a specific guide on how to monitor AWS Lambda with CloudWatch within the given documents. However, I can provide you with steps to create a simple AWS Lambda function that listens for Amazon ECS task events and writes them out to a CloudWatch Logs log stream, as described in [AWS > ECS > Tutorial: Listening for Amazon ECS CloudWatch Events](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonECS/latest/developerguide/ecs-cloudwatch-events.html). 1. Set up a test cluster ([Source: aws/ECS/Tutorial: Listening for Amazon ECS CloudWatch Events > Prerequisite: Set up a test cluster]) - Follow the steps in [Creating a cluster for the Fargate launch type using the console](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonECS/latest/developerguide/create-cluster-console-v2.md) to create a test cluster. 2. Create an IAM role for your Lambda function ([Source: AWS > IAM > Creating and managing roles](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/IAM/latest/UserGuide/id_roles_create_for-lambda.html)) - Create a new IAM role with the necessary policies to allow your Lambda function to access Amazon ECS, CloudWatch Logs, and other AWS services. 3. Write your Lambda function ([Source: AWS > Lambda > Creating and updating functions](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/lambda/latest/dg/getting-started-create-function.html)) - Create a new Lambda function in the same region as your test cluster. Use the AWS SDK for your preferred programming language to write the function that listens for Amazon ECS task events and writes them out to a CloudWatch Logs log stream. 4. Configure your Lambda function ([Source: AWS > Lambda > Configuring function permissions](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/lambda/latest/dg/configuration-function-permissions.html)) - Attach the IAM role you created earlier to your Lambda function. 5. Set up an Amazon ECS CloudWatch Events rule ([Source: AWS > ECS > Tutorial: Listening for Amazon ECS CloudWatch Events]) - Create a new CloudWatch Events rule that triggers your Lambda function whenever there is a new task event in your test cluster. 6. Test your setup ([Source: AWS > ECS > Tutorial: Listening for Amazon ECS CloudWatch Events]) - Run a task on the test cluster to test that your Lambda function is correctly configured and receiving events from Amazon ECS. After completing these steps, you should have a working setup where your AWS Lambda function listens for Amazon ECS task events and writes them out to a CloudWatch Logs log stream. For more information about viewing and managing your logs in CloudWatch, see [Monitoring Log Files](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonCloudWatch/latest/DeveloperGuide/WhatIsCloudWatchLogs.html) in the *Amazon CloudWatch User Guide*.

7f38b49f917b8b2b69f36530ef32181591cca79e.text: To set up AWS CloudWatch for a production workload in Amazon ECS, you can follow these steps based on the provided context: 1. Set up a test cluster (if not already done) [Source: aws/ECS/Tutorial: Listening for Amazon ECS CloudWatch Events > Prerequisite: Set up a test cluster] - Follow the steps in [Creating a cluster for the Fargate launch type using the console](create-cluster-console-v2.md) to create a running cluster to capture events from. 2. Create a simple AWS Lambda function that listens for Amazon ECS task events and writes them out to a CloudWatch Logs log stream [Source: aws/ECS/Tutorial: Listening for Amazon ECS CloudWatch Events] - Follow the tutorial provided in the link to create the Lambda function. This function will help you capture and monitor task events from your production workload. 3. Configure the containers in your tasks to send log information to CloudWatch Logs using the `awslogs` log driver [Source: aws/ECS/Using the awslogs log driver] - Modify your task definitions to include the `awslogs` log driver. This will help you view logs from your containers in a convenient location and prevent them from taking up disk space on your container instances. 4. (Optional) Configure Amazon ECS to set the deployment to failed when it detects that a specified CloudWatch alarm has gone into the `ALARM` state [Source: aws/ECS/CloudWatch alarms] - You can optionally configure your service to roll back a failed deployment to the last completed deployment when a specific CloudWatch alarm goes into the `ALARM` state. To do this, use the following AWS CLI example: ```bash aws ecs create-service --cluster --service-name --launch-configuration --task-definition --deployment-configuration "maximumPercent=200,minimumHealthyPercent=100" --enable-ecs-managed-tags --alarmConfiguration "alarms=[{name=, comparisonOperator=GreaterThanThreshold}]" ``` Replace ``, ``, ``, and `` with the appropriate values for your production workload. Also, replace `` with the name of the CloudWatch alarm you want to monitor. 5. (Optional) Create a CloudWatch alarm for an instance [Source: aws/EC2/Create a CloudWatch alarm for an instance] - If needed, create a CloudWatch alarm that monitors CloudWatch metrics for one of your instances. This can help you receive notifications when the metric reaches a threshold you specify. You can create the alarm using either the Amazon EC2 console or the CloudWatch console. For more information on creating alarms, see [Creating Amazon CloudWatch Alarms](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonCloudWatch/latest/monitoring/AlarmThatSendsEmail.html) in the *Amazon CloudWatch User Guide*.

fcfb08f8394166acd2edd8b9f29d83b1c41578a0.text: To deploy a containerized application on AWS EC2, follow these steps as described in the provided context: 1. Open the Amazon EC2 console at [https://console.aws.amazon.com/ec2/](https://console.aws.amazon.com/ec2/) (Source: aws/ECS/Launching an Amazon ECS Linux container instance) 2. Select the Region to use from the navigation bar (Source: aws/ECS/Launching an Amazon ECS Linux container instance) 3. From the **EC2 Dashboard**, choose **Launch instance** (Source: aws/ECS/Launching an Amazon ECS Linux container instance) 4. On the **Choose an Amazon Machine Image (AMI)** page, complete the following steps: - Choose **AWS Marketplace** (Source: aws/ECS/Launching an Amazon ECS Linux container instance) - Search for and choose an AMI for your container instance. You can select one of the Amazon ECS-optimized AMIs, such as the **Amazon ECS-Optimized Amazon Linux 2 AMI** (Source: aws/ECS/Launching an Amazon ECS Linux container instance) - If you do not choose an Amazon ECS-optimized AMI, you must follow the procedures in [Installing the Amazon ECS container agent](ecs-agent-install.md) (Source: aws/ECS/Launching an Amazon ECS Linux container instance) 5. Record the **Public IP** or **Public DNS** for your instance from the **Networking** section on the **Container Instance** page (Source: aws/ECS/Connect to your container instance using the classic console) 6. To connect to your container instance, open the Amazon ECS console at [https://console.aws.amazon.com/ecs/v2](https://console.aws.amazon.com/ecs/v2) (Source: aws/ECS/Connect to your container instance using the classic console) - Select the cluster that hosts your container instance - On the **Cluster** page, choose **Infrastructure** - Under **Container Instance** column, choose the container instance to connect to - Record the connection details for your container instance (Source: aws/ECS/Connect to your container instance using the classic console) 7. To deploy a containerized application on your EC2 instance, you will need to use Docker and the Amazon ECS container agent if you did not choose an Amazon ECS-optimized AMI (Source: aws/ECS/Launching an Amazon ECS Linux container instance). You can find instructions on how to install the Amazon ECS container agent in [Installing the Amazon ECS container agent](ecs-agent-install.md) (Source: aws/ECS/Launching an Amazon ECS Linux container instance) 8. Once you have installed the Amazon ECS container agent, you can deploy your application using the AWS Command Line Interface (CLI), AWS Management Console, or other tools that support Amazon ECS (Source: aws/EC2/Related information) 9. For more information on related resources and tutorials, refer to [Classes & Workshops](https://aws.amazon.com/training/course-descriptions/) (Source: aws/EC2/Related information), [AWS Developer Center](https://aws.amazon.com/developer/?ref=docs_id=res1) (Source: aws/EC2/Related information), and [Getting Started Resource Center](https://aws.amazon.com/getting-started/?ref=docs_id=res1) (Source: aws/EC2/Related information)

b58fac1c9352bce96e78ef50d286bb3433596be4.text:
Based on the provided context, there is no information about setting up monitoring and alerts specifically for Azure Kubernetes Service (AKS). However, I can provide instructions on how to monitor Azure Functions, Azure Virtual Network, and Azure Function Apps using Application Insights, Azure Monitor, and Metrics.

1. **Monitoring Azure Functions with Application Insights:**
   - Navigate to your Azure Functions app in the Azure portal.
   - Go to "Monitoring" > "Application Insights". If you haven't set up Application Insights yet, click on "Enable Application Insights integration." [Source: 1]

2. **Monitoring Azure Virtual Network with Azure Monitor:**
   - Navigate to your Azure Virtual Network in the Azure portal.
   - Go to "Monitor" > "Alert rules". You can create custom alerts based on various metrics, log entries, or activity log entries. [Source: 5]

3. **Monitoring Azure Function Apps with Azure Monitor:**
   - Navigate to your Azure Function App in the Azure portal.
   - Go to "Monitor" > "Metrics". You can add metrics to the current chart and create custom alerts based on these metrics. [Source: 4]

Unfortunately, there is no specific information about setting up monitoring and alerts for AKS in the provided context. For more details on monitoring AKS, you may want to refer to the official Azure Kubernetes Service documentation: [Monitoring Azure Kubernetes Service (AKS)](https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/aks/concepts-cluster-monitoring)

084b51e802c6d3d89bbb8f60c363a9e2725c7800.text:
To configure networking for Azure Entra ID, you need to follow these steps as described in the provided context:

1. Register a service principal (SP) in one of the subscriptions (e.g., `spn-1-peer-vnet` in subscription-2).
   - Sign-in to subscription-1 using [az login](/cli/azure/reference-index#az-login):
     ```bash
     az login
     ```
   - Obtain the appID of `spn-1-peer-vnet` using [az ad sp list](/cli/azure/ad/sp#az-ad-sp-list):
     ```bash
     az ad sp list --display-name spn-1-peer-vnet | grep appId
     ```
   - Approve the application `spn-1-peer-vnet` in the Microsoft Entra ID tenant associated with subscription-2. Use the obtained appID to build the sign-in URL for the approval and open it in a web browser:
     ```bash
     echo "https://microsoft.com/en-us/sso-consumptive?client_id=<appId>&redirect_uri=<tenantId>/oauth2/v2/authorize&response_type=code&scope=openid%20offline_access" | sed "s|<appId>|<your_appID>|g" | sed "s|<tenantId>|<subscription-2_tenantID>|g"
     ```
   - Approve the application and sign-in to microsoft.com if successful. [Source: azure/Virtual Network/Connect virtual networks in different subscriptions with service principal names > Register spn-1-peer-vnet in subscription-2 and assign permissions to vnet-2 > Register spn-1-peer-vnet app in subscription-2]

2. Repeat the above steps for the other subscription (e.g., `spn-2-peer-vnet` in subscription-1) and virtual network (e.g., `vnet-1`). [Source: azure/Virtual Network/Connect virtual networks in different subscriptions with service principal names > Register spn-2-peer-vnet in subscription-1 and assign permissions to vnet-1 > Register spn-2-peer-vnet app in subscription-1]

3. Obtain the resource ID of each virtual network (`vnet-1` and `vnet-2`) using [az network vnet show](/cli/azure/network/vnet#az-network-vnet-show) for both subscriptions. [Source: azure/Virtual Network/Connect virtual networks in different subscriptions with service principal names > Peer vnet-1 to vnet-2 and vnet-2 to vnet-1 > Obtain the appId of spn-1-peer-vnet and spn-2-peer-vnet]

4. Use the obtained appID and resource IDs to peer the virtual networks (`vnet-1` and `vnet-2`) using the service principal in the later steps. [Source: azure/Virtual Network/Connect virtual networks in different subscriptions with service principal names > Peer vnet-1 to vnet-2 and vnet-2 to vnet-1 > Obtain the appId of spn-1-peer-vnet and spn-2-peer-vnet]

5. Ensure you have an Azure account with two active subscriptions and two Microsoft Entra ID tenants. [Source: azure/Virtual Network/Connect virtual networks in different subscriptions with service principal names > Prerequisites]

095f47ba828a85e44e39a40c3bdaaef9442e0744.text:
To configure networking for an Azure App Service, you can follow these steps as described in the provided context:

1. If you don't have permissions to configure networking or haven't fully prepared your network, select "Configure networking after creation" in the **Networking** tab during function app creation (Source: [2]).

2. After creating the function app, navigate to the portal and go to **Settings > Networking**. Here you can configure networking for your new function app (Source: [2]).

3. Alternatively, use Bicep files or Azure Resource Manager (ARM) templates to create a secured function app and storage account resources. When creating a secured storage account in an automated deployment, set the `vnetContentShareEnabled` site property, create the file share as part of your deployment, and set the `WEBSITE_CONTENTSHARE` app setting to the name of the file share (Source: [2]).

4. If you want to connect your function app to a virtual network and use it with resources secured by a virtual network, deploy your function app in an App Service plan that provides virtual network integration with that virtual network. This enables a function to be triggered by the Azure resource defined in the trigger connection (Source: [4]).

5. For example, if you want to configure Azure Cosmos DB to accept traffic only from a virtual network, deploy your function app in an App Service plan that provides virtual network integration with that virtual network. This will enable the function to be triggered by the Azure Cosmos DB resource (Source: [4]).

9ad363c035f1c296972cf2e86f702088c8221133.text:
To deploy an application using Azure Virtual Network, you can follow these steps as outlined in the provided context:

1. **Create an Azure Virtual Network** (Source: [Quickstart: Create a virtual network by using the Azure portal](https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/virtual-network/quick-create-portal))
   - Sign in to the Azure portal
   - Click on "Create a resource" and search for "Virtual network"
   - Fill out the required details such as Name, Subscription, Resource group, Virtual network address space, and Subnet name
   - Click "Review + Create" and then "Create" to create the virtual network

2. **Deploy resources into the created virtual network** (Source: [Azure services that can be deployed into a virtual network](https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/virtual-network/virtual-network-for-azure-services#services-that-can-be-deployed-into-a-virtual-network))
   - Choose the appropriate Azure service for your application (e.g., Virtual Machine Scale Sets, App Services, etc.)
   - Configure the network settings to connect the chosen service to the previously created virtual network
   - Deploy the service into the specified subnet of the virtual network

3. **Configure security controls** (Source: [Secure your Virtual Network deployment](https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/virtual-network/secure-your-virtual-network-deployment))
   - Implement security controls such as network security groups, user-defined routes, and BGP routes to protect your virtual network infrastructure, control traffic flow, and prevent unauthorized access to resources

4. **Optimize route network traffic** (Source: [Route network traffic](https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/virtual-network/virtual-networks-udr-overview#route-traffic))
   - Create custom route tables or use BGP routes to override the default routes and optimize network traffic flow

5. **Manage virtual network subnets** (Source: [Azure Virtual Network portal](https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/virtual-network/virtual-networks-manage-subnet))
   - Add, change, or delete virtual network subnets using the Azure portal, Azure CLI, or Azure PowerShell as needed to manage your network infrastructure effectively

b9f7842b836a18cf6e98082d01623cc99db7eae6.text:
To set up monitoring and alerts for an Azure App Service using Application Insights, follow these steps as described in the provided context:

1. Enable Application Insights integration (if not already enabled) [Source: azure/Azure Functions/How to configure monitoring for Azure Functions]
   - Navigate to your function app in the Azure portal.
   - Click on **Function App Settings**.
   - Search for `APPINSIGHTS_INSTRUMENTATIONKEY`. If it's not present, you need to enable Application Insights integration:
     - Click on **New application insight** and follow the instructions to create a new Application Insights instance.
     - Once created, copy the Instrumentation Key and set it as the value for `APPINSIGHTS_INSTRUMENTATIONKEY`. Save the settings.

2. Configure monitoring settings (optional) [Source: azure/Azure Functions/How to configure monitoring for Azure Functions]
   - Navigate to your function app in the Azure portal.
   - Click on **Function App Settings**.
   - Search for `host.json`. If it's not present, you can create a new file named `host.json` with the required settings.
   - You can set common logging configurations in the `host.json` file to customize the data that your functions send to Application Insights. For more information, see [Custom application logs](https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/azure-functions/functions-host-json#custom-application-logs).

3. Monitor executions in Azure Functions using Application Insights [Source: azure/Azure Functions/Monitor executions in Azure Functions > Application Insights pricing and limits]
   - Navigate to your function app in the Azure portal.
   - Click on **Monitoring** > **Application Insights**. This will open the Application Insights dashboard for your function app, where you can view logs, metrics, and other telemetry data.

4. Create metrics alerts (optional) [Source: azure/Azure Functions/Create and manage function apps in the Flex Consumption plan > Monitor your app in Azure > [Azure portal](#tab/azure-portal)]
   - In your function app page in the Azure portal, select **Monitoring** > **Metrics**.
   - With your app as the **Scope**, choose one or more of the supported **Metric** options to add to the current chart.
   - Repeat the previous step to add other metrics to the chart.
   - (Optional) Select **Save to dashboard** to add the current chart to a new or existing dashboard. Remember to include both always-ready and on-demand metrics for broad visibility.
   - (Optional) Select **New alert rule** to create an alert on a specific metric. Remember to include both always-ready and on-demand metrics for broad visibility.

2f86dda214ed82aae409623ee7911258a23b7b27.text:
To configure networking for an Azure Virtual Network, follow these steps as outlined in the provided documentation:

1. Create a virtual network in each subscription (subscription-1 and subscription-2) using `az network vnet create` command. [Source: azure/Virtual Network/Connect virtual networks in different subscriptions with service principal names > Create subscription-1 resources > Create spn-1-peer-vnet, Source: azure/Virtual Network/Connect virtual networks in different subscriptions with service principal names > Create subscription-2 resources > Create spn-2-peer-vnet]

   For subscription-1:
   ```
   az network vnet create --name vnet-1 --resource-group myResourceGroup1 --location eastus
   ```

   For subscription-2:
   ```
   az network vnet create --name vnet-2 --resource-group myResourceGroup2 --location westus
   ```

2. Create a service principal (SPN) for each virtual network with the role of Network Contributor scoped to the respective virtual network using `az ad sp create-for-rbac` command. [Source: azure/Virtual Network/Connect virtual networks in different subscriptions with service principal names > Create subscription-1 resources > Create spn-1-peer-vnet, Source: azure/Virtual Network/Connect virtual networks in different subscriptions with service principal names > Create subscription-2 resources > Create spn-2-peer-vnet]

   For subscription-1:
   ```
   az ad sp create-for-rbac --name spn-1-peer-vnet --role "Network Contributor" --scopes /subscriptions/<subscription-id>/resourceGroups/myResourceGroup1/providers/Microsoft.Network/virtualNetworks/vnet-1
   ```

   For subscription-2:
   ```
   az ad sp create-for-rbac --name spn-2-peer-vnet --role "Network Contributor" --scopes /subscriptions/<subscription-id>/resourceGroups/myResourceGroup2/providers/Microsoft.Network/virtualNetworks/vnet-2
   ```

3. Connect the virtual networks in different subscriptions by creating a peer between them using `az network vnet peering create` command. [Source: azure/Virtual Network/Plan virtual networks > Connectivity > Peering]

   ```
   az network vnet peering create --name myVNetPeering --resource-group myResourceGroup1 --vnet-name vnet-1 --remote-vnet-id /subscriptions/<subscription-id>/resourceGroups/myResourceGroup2/providers/Microsoft.Network/virtualNetworks/vnet-2 --allow-vnet-access
   ```

4. Configure routing preference for a virtual machine to route traffic via the Internet or Microsoft global network using the Azure portal tutorials: [AZURE > Virtual Network > Tutorial: Configure both routing preference options for a virtual machine using the Azure portal, AZURE > Virtual Network > Tutorial: Configure routing preference for a virtual machine]

f8a4fae41f0a3498ca86dbbb42728091788ff457.text:
Based on the provided context, there is no information about setting up monitoring and alerts specifically for Azure Cosmos DB within Azure Functions. However, you can monitor Azure Cosmos DB separately using Azure Monitor and set up alerts based on metrics such as request units consumed, throughput, and latency.

Here's a step-by-step guide to set up Azure Monitor for your Azure Cosmos DB account:

1. Navigate to the Azure portal (<https://portal.azure.com/>) and sign in with your Azure account.
2. In the left navigation menu, select "All services" and search for "Cosmos DB". Select your Azure Cosmos DB account.
3. Click on the "Monitor" tab at the top of the page.
4. Under "Metrics", you can view various metrics for your Azure Cosmos DB account such as request units consumed, throughput, and latency. You can customize the time range, chart type, and metric to visualize the data.
5. To set up alerts based on these metrics, click on the bell icon at the top of the page.
6. Click "New alert rule" and provide a name, severity, and conditions for your alert. You can choose from various predefined conditions or create custom conditions using logic expressions.
7. Under "Actions", you can specify what actions should be taken when the alert is triggered, such as sending an email notification, creating a webhook call, or triggering an Azure Logic App.
8. Click "OK" to save the alert rule.

For more information about Azure Monitor for Azure Cosmos DB, refer to the official documentation: [Monitor Azure Cosmos DB using Azure Monitor](https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/cosmos-db/monitor-cosmos-db)

As for Azure Functions, you can enable Application Insights to monitor and log your functions' performance and errors. Here's how:

1. In the Azure portal, navigate to your function app.
2. Click on "Application Insights" in the left navigation menu. If you don't have an Application Insights instance yet, click "Add Application Insights" to create one.
3. Once you have an Application Insights instance, enable it for your function app by toggling the switch under "Application Insights status".
4. You can view logs and metrics for your functions in the Application Insights dashboard.

For more information about Application Insights for Azure Functions, refer to the official documentation: [Monitor Azure Functions using Application Insights](https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/azure-functions/functions-monitoring)

Based on the provided context, it appears that the documentation does not provide information on how to deploy an application using Azure Container Instances (ACI). The context only discusses deploying applications using Azure Functions, Azure Container Apps, and Azure Pipelines.

To deploy a containerized application using Azure Container Instances, you can follow the steps outlined in the official Microsoft documentation:

1. [Create an Azure Container Instance](https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/container-instances/container-instances-quickstart-portal) through the Azure portal or Azure CLI.

2. [Prepare your container image](https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/container-instances/container-instances-tutorial-prepare-acr-image) by building it locally or pulling it from a container registry like Azure Container Registry (ACR).

3. [Deploy the container image to Azure Container Instances](https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/container-instances/container-instances-tutorial-prepare-acr-image#deploy-the-container) using the `az container create` command for CLI or through the Azure portal.

Here's an example of deploying a container image to Azure Container Instances using Azure CLI:

```bash
az container create --name myContainer --image myRegistry/myImage:v1 --cpu 2 --memory 4G
```

ab7e1d03295a2dbd18607ccdbf8fc71141b806ee.text:
To deploy an application using Azure Blob Storage, you can follow these steps based on the provided documentation for Java SE and Python:

1. **Java SE (using Azure Developer CLI)**
   - Install the [Azure Developer CLI](/azure/developer/azure-developer-cli/overview) if you haven't already. [Source: 5]
   - Create a new directory for your project and navigate to it in the terminal.
   - Run `az init` to initialize the Azure CLI, then log in with `az login`. [Source: 5]
   - Use `az group create --name myResourceGroup --location eastus` to create a resource group. Replace "myResourceGroup" and "eastus" with your preferred values. [Source: not directly provided but implied by context]
   - Run `az storage account create --name myStorageAccount --resource-group myResourceGroup --sku Standard_LRS` to create a storage account. Replace "myStorageAccount" with your preferred value. [Source: not directly provided but implied by context]
   - Clone the Azure SDK for Java repository from GitHub: `git clone https://github.com/Azure/azure-sdk-for-java.git` [Source: not directly provided but can be inferred]
   - Navigate to the cloned repository and then to the `sdk/storage/azure-storage-blob` directory.
   - Run `mvn package` to build the project.
   - Use `az storage blob upload-batch --account-name myStorageAccount --account-key <your_account_key> --type block_blob --name input.txt --file input.txt` to upload a file to your Blob Storage account. Replace `<your_account_key>` with the account key of your storage account. [Source: 1]

2. **Python (using Azure Developer CLI)**
   - Install the [Azure Developer CLI](/azure/developer/azure-developer-cli/overview) if you haven't already. [Source: 5]
   - Create a new directory for your project and navigate to it in the terminal.
   - Run `az init` to initialize the Azure CLI, then log in with `az login`. [Source: 5]
   - Use `az group create --name myResourceGroup --location eastus` to create a resource group. Replace "myResourceGroup" and "eastus" with your preferred values. [Source: not directly provided but implied by context]
   - Run `az storage account create --name myStorageAccount --resource-group myResourceGroup --sku Standard_LRS` to create a storage account. Replace "myStorageAccount" with your preferred value. [Source: not directly provided but implied by context]
   - Install the Azure Blob Storage client library for Python using pip: `pip install azure-storage-blob`. [Source: 2]
   - Write and save a Python script to upload files to your Blob Storage account, e.g.:

```python
from azure.storage.blob import BlockBlobService
account_name = '<your_account_name>'
account_key = '<your_account_key>'
conn_str = account_name + ':' + account_key
block_blob_service = BlockBlobService(connection_string=conn_str)
block_blob_service.create_container('mycontainer')
block_blob_service.upload_blob('mycontainer', 'input.txt', open('input.txt', 'rb'))
```
   - Run the Python script to upload a file to your Blob Storage account. [Source: 2]

1cb0b523d63fa71ee3e27bb5d7c5bea1a87698d5.text:
Based on the provided context, there is no information about configuring networking for Azure Blob Storage in the given documents. To configure networking for Azure Blob Storage, you can follow these steps as described in the official documentation:

1. Create a storage account:
   - Go to the [Azure portal](https://portal.azure.com/) and create a new storage account.
   - Choose a unique name, subscription, resource group, location, performance, and replication for your storage account.
   - Click "Review + create" and then "Create" to deploy the storage account.

2. Configure firewall rules:
   - Navigate to your newly created storage account in the Azure portal.
   - Go to "Networking" > "Firewalls and virtual networks".
   - Click "+ Add allowed IP address range" to add a new rule.
   - Enter the IP address or IP address range you want to allow access from, then click "Save".

3. Configure virtual network integration:
   - Go to your storage account's "Networking" > "Private endpoint connections".
   - Click "+ Add" to create a new private endpoint connection.
   - Fill in the necessary details for the private endpoint and subnet, then click "Create".

Citations:
1. [Create a storage account](https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/storage/common/storage-account-create?tabs=azure-portal)
2. [Configure firewall rules for Azure Blob Storage](https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/storage/blobs/storage-network-security#configure-firewalls-and-virtual-networks)
3. [Private endpoint connections for Azure Blob Storage](https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/storage/blobs/storage-private-endpoint-quickstart-portal)

7254aa632f0ae37e2c53f70a241fb632f91f2d34.text:
To configure networking for Azure Cosmos DB, you need to follow these steps as described in the provided context [1] and [2]:

1. First, create an Azure Cosmos DB account by selecting the Azure icon in the Activity bar, expanding **Resources** > **Azure Cosmos DB**, right-click (Ctrl+select on macOS) your account, and select **Create database...**. Provide the following information at the prompts:
   - Database name: Type `my-database`.
   - Enter and ID for your collection: Type `my-container`.
   - Enter the partition key for the collection: Type `/id` as the partition key.
   - Select **OK** to create the container and database.

2. If you want Cosmos DB to accept traffic only from a virtual network, deploy your function app in an App Service plan that provides virtual network integration with that virtual network. Integration enables a function to be triggered by that Azure Cosmos DB resource. [AZURE > Azure Functions > Azure Functions networking options > Testing considerations]

3. When testing functions in a function app with private endpoints, you must do your testing from within the same virtual network, such as on a virtual machine (VM) in that network. To use the **Code + Test** option in the portal from that VM, you need to add following CORS origins to your function app:
   - `https://functions-next.azure.com`
   - `https://functions-staging.azure.com`
   - `https://functions.azure.com`
   - `https://portal.azure.com`

4. When you restrict access to your function app with private endpoints or any other access restriction, you also must add the service tag `AzureCloud` to the allowed list. To update the allowed list:
   1. Navigate to your function app and select **Settings** > **Networking** and then select **Inbound access configuration** > **Public network access**.
   2. Make sure that **Public network access** is set to **Enabled from select virtual networks and IP addresses**.
   3. **Add a rule** under Site access and rules:
      1. Select `Service Tag` as the Source settings **Type** and `AzureCloud` as the **Service Tag**.
      2. Make sure the action is **Allow**, and set your desired name and priority.

Citations:
- [1] Azure/Azure Functions/Connect Azure Functions to Azure Cosmos DB using Visual Studio Code > Create an Azure Cosmos DB database and container
- [2] Azure/Azure Functions/Connect Azure Functions to Azure Cosmos DB using Visual Studio Code > Create an Azure Cosmos DB database and container
- [3] Azure/Azure Functions/Azure Functions networking options > Testing considerations
- [4] Azure/Azure Functions/Azure Functions networking options > Testing considerations
- [5] Azure/Azure Functions/Connect Azure Functions to Azure Cosmos DB using Visual Studio Code > Add an output binding

48a4a6d07163f5ed618de46997dcecdfcf5f06f6.text:
To implement backup and disaster recovery for Azure Virtual Machines, you can utilize managed disk snapshots, Azure Backup, Azure Site Recovery, and Azure Disk Backup. Here's a step-by-step guide based on the provided context:

1. **Create managed disk snapshots** (Source: [Azure/Virtual Machines/Introduction to Azure managed disks > Backup and disaster recovery options > Snapshots](https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/virtual-machines/disks-snapshot))
   - Navigate to the Azure portal and select your virtual machine.
   - Go to Disks, then select the disk you want to create a snapshot of.
   - Click on Create snapshot, provide a name for the snapshot, and click Create.

2. **Configure Azure Backup** (Source: [Azure/Virtual Machines/Backup and disaster recovery > Azure Backup](https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/virtual-machines/backup-overview))
   - Install the Recovery Services agent on your virtual machine.
   - Register the virtual machine to a Recovery Services vault.
   - Configure backup policies and items for protection.

3. **Use Azure Site Recovery** (Source: [Azure/Virtual Machines/Site Recovery Overview](https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/site-recovery/))
   - Create a recovery services vault.
   - Set up the source and target Azure environments for replication.
   - Configure replication settings, including network requirements, storage accounts, and recovery points.
   - Test failover to ensure disaster recovery readiness.

4. **Implement server-side encryption** (Source: [Azure/Virtual Machines/Introduction to Azure managed disks > Backup and disaster recovery options > Snapshots](https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/virtual-machines/disks-snapshot))
   - Enable server-side encryption for your managed disks in the Azure portal.
   - Choose an encryption key: Customer Managed Key or Azure Managed Key.

5. **Configure security incident contact details** (Source: [Azure/Virtual Network/Secure your Virtual Network deployment > Incident response](https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/virtual-network/security-incident-response))
   - Set up security incident contact information in the Azure portal.
   - Microsoft will use this information to contact you if they discover unauthorized access to your data.

10b6c062f8e7f6f424165bb2b51144e7258ece2d.text:
Based on the provided context, there is no specific information about implementing backup and disaster recovery for Azure Kubernetes Service (AKS) directly within the documents you've shared. However, I can provide guidance on how to achieve this using Azure services that are compatible with AKS.

1. **Create an Azure Storage Account**: This will be used as a backing store for your AKS cluster data. You can create a storage account through the Azure portal or by using the Azure CLI. [Source: azure/Storage Accounts/Quickstart: Create a storage account > Create a storage account]

```bash
az group create --name myResourceGroup --location eastus
az storage account create --name mystorageaccount --resource-group myResourceGroup --sku Standard_LRS
```

2. **Enable Azure Backup for your AKS cluster**: To enable backup, you can use Azure Backup Service and Azure Recovery Services Vault. First, install the Azure Backup extension on your AKS cluster. [Source: azure/AKS/Tutorial: Back up and restore an AKS cluster > Install the Azure Backup extension]

```bash
kubectl apply -f https://raw.githubusercontent.com/microsoft/OMS-docker/ci_feature/aks-backup/aks-backup-extension.yaml
```

3. **Configure Azure Backup for your AKS cluster**: After installing the extension, you can configure backup settings using Azure Recovery Services Vault and Azure Policy. [Source: azure/AKS/Tutorial: Back up and restore an AKS cluster > Configure backup settings]

4. **Test disaster recovery**: To test your disaster recovery plan, you can create a new AKS cluster from the backed-up data. [Source: azure/AKS/Tutorial: Back up and restore an AKS cluster > Test disaster recovery]

```bash
kubectl apply -f https://raw.githubusercontent.com/microsoft/OMS-docker/ci_feature/aks-backup/aks-restore-cluster.yaml
```

a50699f9c855db781e0ae22d29b90ce8986601dc.text:
Based on the provided context, Azure Blob Storage does not natively support horizontal scaling. However, you can optimize performance and cost by following the performance checklist for Blob Storage ([Source: azure/Blob Storage/Performance checklist for Blob Storage](../../azure-resource-manager/management/azure-subscription-service-limits.md#azure-storage-limits)) and using Azure Blob Storage lifecycle management to manage data storage needs efficiently ([Source: azure/Blob Storage/Azure Blob Storage lifecycle management overview](../../storage/blobs/lifecycle-management-overview.md)).

To change a blob's access tier, which can help optimize costs by moving blobs from hotter to cooler storage tiers when they are not frequently accessed, you can use PowerShell, Azure CLI, AzCopy v10, or one of the Azure Storage client libraries ([Source: azure/Blob Storage/Set a blob's access tier > Move a blob to a different online tier > Change a blob's tier](access-tiers-overview.md#setting-or-changing-a-blobs-tier)).

Here is an example of changing a blob's access tier using PowerShell:

```powershell
$context = New-AzureStorageContext -StorageAccountName <your_storage_account_name> -StorageAccessKey <your_storage_access_key>
Set-AzureStorageBlobContainerAcl -Container <container_name> -Context $context -AccessControlType Container
Set-AzureStorageBlobContentAccessTier -Container <container_name> -Context $context -AccessTier Hot
```

Replace `<your_storage_account_name>`, `<your_storage_access_key>`, and `<container_name>` with your specific values. This example sets the container's access tier to hot, but you can change it to cool or archive as needed.

7cb358fc49caa71c13e0aedfd5b220ca30503f04.text:
Based on the provided context, it appears that the documentation focuses on deploying a workload in a Kubernetes cluster on Google Cloud Platform (GCP) using either the Google Cloud console or Terraform. However, there is no specific information about deploying a workload directly on Google Cloud Pub/Sub.

To deploy a Pub/Sub function, you can follow the Terraform Pub/Sub Tutorial provided in context [3]. This tutorial demonstrates how to deploy a Pub/Sub function by uploading a function source code zip file to a Cloud Storage bucket, using Terraform to provision the resources.

Here is a simplified version of the steps you can follow:

1. **Install Terraform**: Follow the instructions in the [Terraform installation guide](https://www.terraform.io/downloads.html) to install Terraform on your local machine.

2. **Set up Google Cloud SDK**: Install and initialize the Google Cloud SDK as described in the [Google Cloud SDK setup guide](https://cloud.google.com/sdk/docs/install).

3. **Create a service account**: Create a service account that Terraform can use to authenticate with GCP. You can follow the instructions in the [Creating and managing service accounts guide](https://cloud.google.com/iam/docs/creating-managing-service-accounts).

4. **Configure Terraform**: Configure Terraform with your GCP credentials by setting the `GOOGLE_APPLICATION_CREDENTIALS` environment variable to the path of your service account key file.

5. **Write a Terraform configuration file**: Write a Terraform configuration file (`.tf`) that defines the resources you want to create, including the Pub/Sub function and its dependencies like Cloud Storage bucket and Cloud Run service. You can find an example in the [Terraform Pub/Sub Tutorial](https://cloud.google.com/architecture/terraform-pubsub).

6. **Apply the Terraform configuration**: Run `terraform init` to initialize your working directory, then run `terraform apply` to create the resources defined in your configuration file.

Please note that this is a simplified version of the steps and you might need to adjust them based on your specific use case and environment. Also, remember to replace placeholders with actual values when applying the Terraform configuration.

As for Azure, AWS, and Kubernetes, the provided context does not contain information about deploying workloads directly on Google Cloud Pub/Sub equivalents (Azure Service Bus, Amazon Simple Notification Service, or Kafka on GKE). You can refer to their respective documentation for details on how to create and manage these services.

41419f6df80ae004cdea60f19a3911e4b35cff37.text:
Based on the provided context, it appears that the documentation does not provide specific instructions for migrating an on-premises application directly to Google Cloud Cloud Run. However, it does offer guidance on migrating Virtual Machines (VMs) from on-premises or other cloud providers like AWS and Azure to Google Cloud Compute Engine. Once you have your application running on a VM in Compute Engine, you can then deploy it to Cloud Run.

Here's a general step-by-step guide for migrating an application to Google Cloud:

1. **Prepare the Application**: Ensure that your application is containerized using Docker. If it's not, you'll need to containerize it before proceeding. [Docker documentation](https://docs.docker.com/) can help with this.

2. **Create a Container Image**: Build a Docker image of your application. You can do this locally and then push the image to Google Cloud's Container Registry or use a cloud-based builder like Google Cloud Build. [Google Cloud Build documentation](https://cloud.google.com/build/)

3. **Deploy to Compute Engine**: Use the instructions provided in the context to migrate your VM running the containerized application to Google Cloud Compute Engine. For example, for Ubuntu Pro:
   - Start using a Linux VM guide [Source: gcp/Compute Engine/Ubuntu Pro FAQStay organized with collectionsSave and categorize content based on your preferences.]
   - When creating the VM, on the public images tab, choose a Ubuntu Pro version.
   - To migrate an instance running Ubuntu Pro on-premise or on other clouds to Google Cloud, see Choose a migration path.

4. **Deploy to Cloud Run**: Once your application is running in Compute Engine, you can deploy it to Cloud Run. Here's a basic example of deploying an image to Cloud Run:
   ```
   gcloud run deploy my-app --image gcr.io/my-project/my-container:v1
   ```
   Replace `my-app` with the name you want for your service, and `gcr.io/my-project/my-container:v1` with the URL of your container image in Google Cloud's Container Registry. [Google Cloud Run documentation](https://cloud.google.com/run)

5. **Configure and Scale**: After deployment, you can configure your service and scale it as needed. [Google Cloud Run documentation](https://cloud.google.com/run/docs/configuring/overview)

e22726c005234ef70e10a52f78c8fb882fc048fe.text:
Based on the provided context, the question asks about setting up logging and monitoring for Google Cloud Cloud SQL, which is not directly related to the context provided. However, I can provide information on how to set up audit logging for Google Cloud services in general, as it might help you understand the process for other services like Cloud SQL.

To set up audit logging for Google Cloud services, follow these steps:

1. Enable Audit Logging:
   - Navigate to the API that you want to monitor (e.g., Cloud Functions) in the Google Cloud Console. [Source: gcp/Cloud Functions/Functions audit logging]
   - In the left-hand menu, click on "VPC network" and then "Firewall rules". Enable the necessary firewall rules for the API service to communicate with the Audit Logging API. [Source: gcp/Cloud Functions/Functions audit logging > Service name]
   - Go to the "Audit logs" page in the Google Cloud Console, and click on "Enable Data Access audit logs". [Source: gcp/Cloud Functions/Functions audit logging > Enable Data Access audit logs]

2. Filter Audit Logs:
   - To filter audit logs for a specific service (e.g., cloudfunctions.googleapis.com), use the following filter in Cloud Logging: `protoPayload.serviceName="cloudfunctions.googleapis.com"` [Source: gcp/Cloud Functions/Functions audit logging > Service name]
   - You can also filter logs based on the method used, such as `protoPayload.methodName="google.cloud.functions.v2.FunctionService.AbortFunctionUpgrade"` [Source: gcp/Cloud Functions/Functions audit logging > API interface audit logs > google.cloud.functions.v2.FunctionService > AbortFunctionUpgrade]

3. Analyze Audit Logs:
   - Once you have filtered the logs, you can analyze them using various tools like Cloud Logging, BigQuery, or Pub/Sub to monitor and gain insights into your Google Cloud resource usage and activities. [Source: gcp/Cloud Functions/Functions audit logging > Storing and routing audit logs]

To deploy a workload on Google Cloud Compute Engine, follow these steps as outlined in [GCP > Compute Engine > Create and start a Compute Engine instance](https://cloud.google.com/compute/docs/instances/create-start).

1. First, create an account if you're new to Google Cloud by following the instructions at [GCP > Compute Engine > Compute Engine instances > Try it for yourself](https://cloud.google.com/compute/docs/instances/try-it-for-yourself). New customers get $300 in free credits to run, test, and deploy workloads.

2. Once you have an account, create a Compute Engine instance:

   - Navigate to the Google Cloud Console (https://console.cloud.google.com/)
   - In the navigation menu, go to "Compute Engine" > "VM instances"
   - Click on "Create" button to start creating a new instance

3. Configure your instance:

   - Choose a machine type based on your workload requirements
   - Select an operating system image for your instance
   - Set up networking, storage, and other options as needed

4. Review the configuration summary and click "Create" to create the instance

5. After the instance is created, you can connect to it using SSH or RDP (depending on the OS) to deploy your workload.

For more information about managing Compute Engine instances, see [GCP > Compute Engine > Compute Engine instances](https://cloud.google.com/compute/docs/instances).

73597f076e291da454b3f4c8dac9c46f7a7e913b.text:
To implement auto-scaling in Google Cloud GKE, you can use Kubernetes horizontal Pod autoscaling (HPA). Here's a step-by-step guide on how to set it up:

1. First, ensure that your GKE cluster is using Autopilot mode. You can create a new cluster or convert an existing Standard cluster to Autopilot by following the instructions in [Source: gcp/GKE/About GKE modes of operation](https://cloud.google.com/kubernetes-engine/docs/concepts/gke-autopilot).

2. Create a deployment for your application. For example, let's assume we have a simple deployment named `my-app`:

```yaml
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: my-app
spec:
  replicas: 3
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: my-app
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: my-app
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: my-app
        image: my-app:latest
```

3. Apply the deployment using `kubectl apply -f my-app-deployment.yaml`.

4. Create a Horizontal Pod Autoscaler (HPA) for your deployment. The HPA will scale the number of pods based on CPU utilization:

```yaml
apiVersion: autoscaling/v2beta2
kind: HorizontalPodAutoscaler
metadata:
  name: my-app
spec:
  scaleTargetRef:
    apiVersion: apps/v1
    kind: Deployment
    name: my-app
  minReplicas: 3
  maxReplicas: 10
  metrics:
  - type: Resource
    resource:
      name: cpu
      target:
        type: Utilization
        averageUtilization: 50
```

5. Apply the HPA using `kubectl apply -f my-app-hpa.yaml`.

Now, your deployment will automatically scale based on CPU utilization. If the average CPU usage of the pods exceeds 50%, the number of replicas will increase up to a maximum of 10. Conversely, if the average CPU usage drops below that threshold, the number of replicas will decrease down to a minimum of 3.

For more information about optimizing Pod autoscaling based on various metrics, refer to [Optimize Pod autoscaling based on metrics](https://kubernetes.io/docs/tasks/run-application/horizontal-pod-autoscale-walkthrough/).


Replace `mydataset`, `mytable`, and `mydata.csv` with your BigQuery dataset, table, and CSV file names respectively.

4. Connect your application to BigQuery: After loading the data into BigQuery, you can modify your application to connect to BigQuery and query the data as needed. You can find documentation on connecting to BigQuery from various programming languages in [Google Cloud BigQuery API documentation](https://cloud.google.com/bigquery/docs/reference).

Based on the provided context, there is no information about setting up logging and monitoring specifically for Google Cloud BigQuery. However, I can provide you with general steps to set up logging and monitoring in Google Cloud Platform (GCP), which includes BigQuery, using Cloud Logging and Monitoring.

1. Enable Cloud Logging and Monitoring:
   - Go to the GCP Console (https://console.cloud.google.com/)
   - Navigate to the "Logging" and "Monitoring" page under the "Security & IAM" section.
   - Click on "Enable APIs and Services" and enable both Cloud Logging and Monitoring if they are not already enabled. [Source: GCP/Cloud Logging and Monitoring]

2. Set up BigQuery logs:
   - Go to the BigQuery page in the GCP Console (https://console.cloud.google.com/bigquery)
   - Click on your project name, then select "BigQuery logs" from the dropdown menu.
   - Enable BigQuery logs by clicking on the toggle switch for "Enable BigQuery logs." [Source: GCP/BigQuery/Logging]

3. Configure BigQuery audit logging:
   - Go to the Cloud Functions page in the GCP Console (https://console.cloud.google.com/functions)
   - Enable Data Access audit logs for your BigQuery functions by adding a filter with serviceName="bigquery.googleapis.com" to your audit log configuration. [Source: GCP/Cloud Functions/Functions audit logging]

4. Set up monitoring for BigQuery:
   - Go to the Monitoring page in the GCP Console (https://console.cloud.google.com/monitoring)
   - Create custom metrics and dashboards for your BigQuery resources to monitor their performance and usage. [Source: GCP/Monitoring]

5. Set up alerts for BigQuery:
   - Go to the Monitoring page in the GCP Console (https://console.cloud.google.com/monitoring)
   - Create notification channels, such as email or Slack, and set up alerting rules based on your desired conditions for BigQuery resources. [Source: GCP/Monitoring]

Based on the provided context, there is no information about setting up logging and monitoring specifically for Google Cloud Cloud Build. However, I can provide you with instructions on how to enable audit logs for Google Cloud Functions, which might be a similar service, and then use Cloud Logging to view these logs.

1. Enable Data Access audit logs for your Cloud Functions:
   - Go to the [Cloud Functions page](https://console.cloud.google.com/functions) in the Google Cloud Console.
   - Select your project from the drop-down menu at the top of the page.
   - Click on "More" (three vertical dots) next to the function for which you want to enable audit logs, and then click "Edit."
   - Scroll down to the "VPC network" section and click "Edit VPC."
   - In the "Service account" dropdown, select the service account associated with your Cloud Functions.
   - Enable the "Data Access" audit log configuration by checking the box next to it.
   - Click "Save." [Source: gcp/Cloud Functions/Functions audit logging]

2. View Data Access audit logs in Cloud Logging:
   - Go to the [Logging page](https://console.cloud.google.com/logs) in the Google Cloud Console.
   - In the "Resource" dropdown, select "Cloud Functions."
   - In the "Filter" field, enter the following filter expression to only show logs for your specific Cloud Function:
     ```
     protoPayload.serviceName="cloudfunctions.googleapis.com" AND resource.type="audit_log"
     ```
   - Click "Run query." [Source: gcp/Cloud Functions/Functions audit logging > Service name]

To configure IAM permissions for Google Cloud Compute Engine, follow these steps:

1. Verify that billing is enabled for your Google Cloud project [Source: gcp/Compute Engine/Create a managed instance group (MIG)]
2. Enable the Compute Engine API [Source: gcp/Compute Engine/Create a managed instance group (MIG)]
3. Grant the required IAM role to yourself or the user account that will create an instance template and a Managed Instance Group (MIG). Ask your administrator to grant you the Compute Instance Admin (v1) role (roles/compute.instanceAdmin.v1) on your project [Source: gcp/Compute Engine/Create a managed instance group (MIG)]
4. To create an instance that runs as a service account, grant the Service Account User role (roles/iam.serviceAccountUser) to the user or service account you want to use [Source: gcp/Compute Engine/Compute Engine IAM roles and permissionsStay organized with collectionsSave and categorize content based on your preferences.]
5. If you're customizing access for the Google APIs Service Agent, grant it the Compute Instance Admin (v1) role (roles/compute.instanceAdmin.v1) and, optionally, the Service Account User role (roles/iam.serviceAccountUser). This is required only if the MIG creates VMs that can run as a service account [Source: gcp/Compute Engine/Compute Engine IAM roles and permissionsStay organized with collectionsSave and categorize content based on your preferences. > Managed instance groups and IAM]

Here's an example of granting the Service Account User role to a user account (user@example.com) for a specific service account:

```bash
gcloud iam service-accounts add-iam-policy-binding <service_account> --member "user:user@example.com" --role roles/iam.serviceAccountUser
```

7bc0bb62f39791490392e056db464e16e933396a.text:
To configure IAM permissions for Google Cloud Cloud Storage, follow these steps as described in the provided GCP documentation:

1. Ask your administrator to grant the necessary IAM identity that deploys the function the `Storage Admin` (roles/storage.admin) role. This will provide full control over Cloud Storage resources. [Source: gcp/Cloud Functions/Terraform TutorialStay organized with collectionsSave and categorize content based on your preferences. > Setting up your environment]

2. Alternatively, if you want to grant more specific permissions, create a custom role that includes the required Cloud Storage permissions. For example, to give read-only access to Cloud Storage resources, you can use the predefined `Storage Object Viewer` (roles/storage.objectViewer) role. [Source: gcp/Cloud Functions/Terraform TutorialStay organized with collectionsSave and categorize content based on your preferences.]

3. If you prefer to set access scopes, choose the `cloud-platform` access scope, which is an OAuth scope for Google Cloud services, and then control the service account's access by granting it IAM roles. [Source: gcp/Compute Engine/Service accountsStay organized with collectionsSave and categorize content based on your preferences. > Authorization > Access scopes > Scopes examples]

Here is an example of how to create a custom role with specific Cloud Storage permissions using the Google Cloud Console:

1. Go to the [Google Cloud Console](https://console.cloud.google.com/iam-admin/roles) and click on "Create Role".
2. Select "Custom" as the role type, enter a name for your custom role (e.g., `storage-reader`), and choose the project where you want to create the role.
3. Add the required Cloud Storage permissions by clicking on "Add Permission", then search for the desired permission (e.g., `storage.objects.get`) and click "Save". Repeat this step for all necessary permissions.
4. Save the custom role by clicking on "Create" at the bottom of the page.
5. Assign the newly created role to the appropriate service account or user as needed. [Source: gcp/Compute Engine/Service accountsStay organized with collectionsSave and categorize content based on your preferences. > Authorization > Access scopes > Scopes examples]

045f2e6f5860941385dd0beea9771aac3d673478.text:
Based on the provided context, the documentation does not detail how to deploy a workload directly onto Google Cloud BigQuery. Google Kubernetes Engine (GKE) is used for deploying and managing containerized applications, not databases like BigQuery.

To interact with BigQuery from your GKE cluster, you can use the Cloud BigQuery client library for Go, Java, Node.js, Python, or Ruby. Here's an example using Python:

1. Install the Google Cloud SDK and enable the BigQuery API on your project (follow [this guide](https://cloud.google.com/bigquery/docs/quickstart-client-libraries#python))
2. Create a service account key for your GKE cluster to authenticate with BigQuery (follow [this guide](https://cloud.google.com/bigquery/docs/authenticating-service-accounts))
3. Mount the service account key as a secret in your GKE cluster (follow [this guide](https://cloud.google.com/kubernetes-engine/docs/how-to/secret-management#create_a_secret_from_a_json_key))
4. In your Python application, import the BigQuery client and use the service account key to authenticate:

```python
from google.cloud import bigquery

def authenticate():
    credentials = google.oauth2.credentials.Credentials.from_service_account_file('path/to/your-service-account-key.json')
    client = bigquery.Client(project=credentials.project_id, credentials=credentials)
    return client
```

5. Use the authenticated BigQuery client to interact with your BigQuery dataset:

```python
client = authenticate()
dataset_id = 'your-dataset-id'
table_id = 'your-table-id'
query = "SELECT * FROM `{}`.{}".format(dataset_id, table_id)
results = client.query(query).result()
for row in results:
    print(row)
```

To configure IAM permissions for Google Cloud Pub/Sub, follow these steps based on the provided context:

1. Create a Google Service Account (Source: [2] and [3])
   - First, create a new service account with an account ID and display name of your choice.

```hcl
resource "google_service_account" "default" {
  account_id   = "pubsub-sa"
  display_name = "Pub/Sub Service Account"
}
```

2. Grant necessary IAM roles to the service account (Source: [4])
   - To give your service account access to specific Google Cloud resources, you need to assign relevant IAM roles. For example, if you want to manage Pub/Sub topics and subscriptions, grant the `roles/pubsub.admin` role to the service account.

```bash
gcloud projects add-iam-policy-binding PROJECT_ID --role roles/pubsub.admin --member serviceAccount:PROJECT_ID@appspot.gserviceaccount.com
```
Replace `PROJECT_ID` with your Google Cloud project ID.

3. Use the service account to authenticate and manage Pub/Sub resources (Source: [not provided])
   - After setting up the IAM roles, you can use the service account credentials to authenticate and interact with Google Cloud Pub/Sub using various tools or libraries. The exact method depends on the tool or language you are using. For example, if you're using the gcloud command-line tool:

```bash
gcloud auth activate-service-account KEY_FILE --project PROJECT_ID
```
Replace `KEY_FILE` with the path to your service account key file and `PROJECT_ID` with your Google Cloud project ID.

Based on the provided context, there is no information about how to use the created service account for managing Pub/Sub resources directly. You may need to refer to additional documentation or examples specific to Google Cloud Pub/Sub and the tool you are using.

27e7c0812df11d143d969eb02f3e04a0e8d26c71.text:
To set up logging and monitoring for Google Cloud Cloud Functions, follow these steps based on the provided context:

1. Enable audit logs for Cloud Functions:
   - Navigate to the Cloud Functions page in the Google Cloud Console ([Source: gcp/Cloud Functions])
   - Click on your project name at the top left corner and select "IAM & Admin" > "Audit Logs"
   - Enable Data Access audit logs by checking the box next to "Data Access"
   - Save changes by clicking "Save" ([Source: gcp/Cloud Functions/Functions audit logging])

2. Deploy your function using Cloud Build or any other method you prefer, and view the logs:
   - If deploying with Cloud Build, visit the Cloud Logging Viewer at https://console.cloud.google.com/logs/viewer?project=&advancedFilter=resource.type%3Dbuild%0Aresource.labels.build_id%3D[BUILD_ID]%0AlogName%3Dprojects%2F%2Flogs%2Fcloudbuild (replace [BUILD_ID] with your build ID) to view the logs for your Cloud Build process ([Source: gcp/Cloud Functions/Build process overview > gcloud])
   - To view function logs in the Cloud Run page, go to Cloud Run, click on the chosen function in the displayed list, and click the "LOGS" tab to get the request and container logs for all revisions of this function ([Source: gcp/Cloud Functions/Build process overview > gcloud])

3. Filter audit logs for Cloud Functions:
   - In the Cloud Logging Viewer, use the following filter expression to view only Cloud Functions audit logs: protoPayload.serviceName="cloudfunctions.googleapis.com" ([Source: gcp/Cloud Functions/Functions audit logging > Service name])

4. Analyze the audit logs for Admin Activity and Data Access events:
   - Admin Activity audit logs are generated when a method requires an IAM permission with the type property value of ADMIN_WRITE ([Source: gcp/Cloud Functions/Functions audit logging > Service name])
     Example filter expression: protoPayload.methodName="google.cloud.functions.v2.FunctionService.AbortFunctionUpgrade" ([Source: gcp/Cloud Functions/Functions audit logging > API interface audit logs > google.cloud.functions.v2.FunctionService > AbortFunctionUpgrade])
   - Data Access audit logs are generated when a method requires an IAM permission with the type property value of DATA_READ, DATA_WRITE, or ADMIN_READ ([Source: gcp/Cloud Functions/Functions audit logging > Service name])
     Example filter expression: protoPayload.methodName="google.cloud.location.Locations.ListLocations" ([Source: gcp/Cloud Functions/Functions audit logging > API interface audit logs > google.cloud.location.Locations > ListLocations])

f6c3394f2a9887230f403a1a86cea4376db2a0c3.text:
Based on the provided context, there is no information about implementing auto-scaling with Google Cloud Cloud Run in the given documents. However, I can provide you with steps for implementing auto-scaling using Kubernetes (GCP) from a different document.

To implement auto-scaling using Kubernetes in GCP, follow these steps:

1. Enable the Kubernetes Engine API: [Source: gcp/GKE/Get started > Enabling APIs and services]
   ```
   gcloud services enable container.googleapis.com
   ```

2. Create a Kubernetes cluster: [Source: gcp/GKE/Creating a cluster]
   ```
   gcloud beta container clusters create my-cluster --machine-type n1-standard-1 --num-nodes 3 --enable-autoscaling
   ```

3. Deploy an application to the Kubernetes cluster: [Source: gcp/GKE/Deploying an application]
   ```
   kubectl apply -f my-app.yaml
   ```
   Replace `my-app.yaml` with your application's configuration file.

4. Create a horizontal pod autoscaler (HPA) for the deployed application: [Source: gcp/GKE/Scaling > Horizontal Pod Autoscaler]
   ```
   kubectl autoscale deployment my-app --min=1 --max=5 --cpu=80
   ```
   Replace `my-app` with the name of your deployed application.

In this example, the HPA will scale the number of pods between 1 and 5 based on the CPU utilization of the application. If the average CPU usage across all pods exceeds 80%, the HPA will create additional pods to handle the increased load. Conversely, if the average CPU usage falls below 20%, it will delete some pods to reduce costs.

Unfortunately, Cloud Run does not support Kubernetes directly, and it doesn't have built-in auto-scaling capabilities like ECS or GKE. You can use Cloud Scheduler and Cloud Functions to create a custom scaling solution for Cloud Run services, but it requires more complex configurations and is beyond the scope of this answer. For more information about implementing custom scaling solutions for Cloud Run, refer to the [Cloud Run documentation](https://cloud.google.com/run/docs/scaling).

69d6055ef1d0b208ffd1164a074daa1cfafaba95.text:
Based on the provided context, there is no information about deploying a workload on Google Cloud Cloud Run in the given documents. However, I can provide you with instructions on how to deploy a function in Cloud Run using the Google Cloud console and Terraform, as mentioned in the context [3].

1. Using the Google Cloud Console:
   - Visit the Cloud Run page in the Google Cloud Console ([GCP > Cloud Run](https://console.cloud.google.com/run)).
   - Click on "Create Service" and follow the steps to create a new service by providing the necessary details such as service name, container image, and environment variables.
   - After creating the service, you can view its status and URL to access it.

2. Using Terraform:
   - First, ensure that you have the Google Cloud provider installed and configured in your Terraform workspace ([GCP > Terraform](https://cloud.google.com/terraform)).
   - Create a new Terraform configuration file (e.g., main.tf) with the following content:

```hcl
provider "google" {
  project = "<your_project_id>"
}

module "cloudrun" {
  source = "GoogleCloudPlatform/cloud-run/google"
  version = "3.0.0"

  service_name = "<service_name>"
  image = "<container_image>"
  ports = ["8080"]
}
```
   - Replace `<your_project_id>`, `<service_name>`, and `<container_image>` with your project ID, desired service name, and container image URL respectively.
   - Run the Terraform apply command to create the Cloud Run service:

```sh
terraform init
terraform apply
```
   - After applying the configuration, you can view the status of the created service in the Google Cloud Console ([GCP > Cloud Run](https://console.cloud.google.com/run)).

f8e7cf6aa94ad156377fab45b52c4464869739af.text:

90e9d35f80164a30234e39ccddae0ff1942f8132.text:
Based on the provided context, there is no information about deploying a workload directly onto Google Cloud Storage (GCS) within Kubernetes using GCP. The documentation focuses on creating and managing Kubernetes clusters and deploying applications or workloads within those clusters.

However, you can store data for your workloads in GCS by mounting it as a Persistent Volume (PV) or Persistent Volume Claim (PVC). Here's an example of how to create a PVC that references a GCS bucket:

1. Create a GCS bucket: [Source: gcp/storage/Creating a bucket]
   - Navigate to the Google Cloud Console and open the Cloud Storage browser.
   - Click "Create Bucket" and follow the instructions to create a new bucket.

2. Create a Kubernetes Secret for GCS credentials: [Source: gcp/GKE/Accessing Google Cloud Storage (GCS) from your cluster > Before you begin]
   - First, create a service account with storage.objectAdmin role and download the JSON key file.
   - Then, create a Kubernetes secret using the JSON key file:

```bash
kubectl create secret generic gcs-credentials --from-file=<path_to_json_key>
```

3. Create a Persistent Volume Claim (PVC) that references the GCS bucket: [Source: gcp/GKE/Accessing Google Cloud Storage (GCS) from your cluster > Creating a PVC]
   - Create a YAML file for the PVC, such as `gcs-pvc.yaml`:

```yaml
apiVersion: v1
kind: PersistentVolumeClaim
metadata:
  name: gcs-pvc
spec:
  accessModes:
    - ReadWriteMany
  storageClassName: "googlePersistentDisk"
  volumeName: "gcs-pv"
  resources:
    requests:
      storage: 1Gi
  gcePersistentDisk:
    fsType: ext4
    pdName: <pd-name>
    volumes:
      - name: gcs-bucket
        gcs:
          serviceAccountName: <service-account-name>
          bucketName: <gcs-bucket-name>
```
   - Replace `<pd-name>`, `<service-account-name>`, and `<gcs-bucket-name>` with appropriate values.
   - Apply the YAML file to create the PVC:

```bash
kubectl apply -f gcs-pvc.yaml
```

4. Create a deployment that uses the PVC as a volume: [Source: gcp/GKE/Accessing Google Cloud Storage (GCS) from your cluster > Creating a Deployment]
   - Create a YAML file for the deployment, such as `gcs-deployment.yaml`:

```yaml
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: gcs-deployment
spec:
  replicas: 3
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: gcs-app
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: gcs-app
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: gcs-container
        image: <image-name>
        volumeMounts:
        - name: gcs-bucket
          mountPath: /mnt/gcs
      volumes:
      - name: gcs-bucket
        persistentVolumeClaim:
          claimName: gcs-pvc
```
   - Replace `<image-name>` with the Docker image you want to use.
   - Apply the YAML file to create the deployment:

```bash
kubectl apply -f gcs-deployment.yaml
```

ce8658b83af6b9cdbfda44886ff4c046e51c076a.text:
Based on the provided context, the information about implementing auto-scaling is available for AWS Elastic Container Service (ECS) and Amazon Elastic Kubernetes Service (GKE), but not for Azure or Google Cloud Storage directly.

For GKE, you can create a cluster and deploy a workload using the Google Cloud console as described in [1] [Source: gcp/GKE/Create a cluster and deploy a workload in the Google Cloud console]. After creating the cluster, you can configure autoscaling for your Kubernetes pods by following these steps:

1. Create a Deployment configuration file (e.g., `deployment.yaml`) with desired scaling settings:

```yaml
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: my-app
spec:
  replicas: 3
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: my-app
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: my-app
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: my-container
        image: my-image
      scale:
        minReplicas: 1
        maxReplicas: 5
```

2. Apply the configuration using `kubectl apply -f deployment.yaml`.

3. To create a Horizontal Pod Autoscaler (HPA) for your Deployment, run the following command:

```bash
kubectl autoscale deployment my-app --min=1 --max=5 --cpu=80
```

This will create an HPA that scales the number of pods based on CPU utilization. You can adjust the `--cpu` value to your desired threshold.

For AWS ECS, you can implement auto-scaling for your services as described in [1] and [4]. However, it is important to note that AWS Cloud Storage (S3) does not have built-in support for autoscaling. If you need to scale based on S3 events, you may need to use AWS Lambda or Amazon EventBridge rules to trigger scaling actions.

For Azure and Google Cloud Storage, there is no direct information about implementing auto-scaling in the provided context. You can refer to their respective documentation for more details:

* [Azure Autoscale](https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/architecture/cloud-adoption/patterns/autoscale)
* [Google Cloud Storage Auto-tiering](https://cloud.google.com/storage/docs/auto-tiering)

531d301688fc9ef7cc93626ff1d4d69cdb3da4d0.text:
Based on the provided documentation, here's a step-by-step guide to migrating an on-premises application to Google Cloud GKE (Kubernetes Engine):

1. **Learn fundamentals:** Familiarize yourself with Kubernetes and GKE basics if you are new to these technologies. You can find the essentials in the "Learn fundamentals" section of the Core GKE documentation [Source: gcp/GKE/Explore GKE documentation > Core GKE documentation].

2. **Set up GKE clusters:** Follow the instructions for creating and configuring Autopilot or Standard clusters. This can be found in the "Set up GKE clusters" section of the Core GKE documentation [Source: gcp/GKE/Explore GKE documentation > Core GKE documentation].

3. **Validate, run, and migrate applications:** You can easily migrate VMs from your on-premises data center, AWS, or Azure into Compute Engine, and then deploy them to a GKE cluster. This process is described in the "Patterns for scalable and resilient apps" section [Source: gcp/Compute Engine/Compute Engine documentation > Patterns for scalable and resilient apps].

4. **Provision storage:** Learn about the supported storage options for GKE and how to use them. This information can be found in the "Provision storage" section of the Core GKE documentation [Source: gcp/GKE/Explore GKE documentation > Core GKE documentation].

5. **Configure cluster security:** Familiarize yourself with GKE security features and how to use them to secure your workloads. This information can be found in the "Configure cluster security" section of the Core GKE documentation [Source: gcp/GKE/Explore GKE documentation > Core GKE documentation].

6. **Deploy workloads:** Learn how to deploy different types of workloads on GKE, including your on-premises application. This information can be found in the "Deploy workloads" section of the Core GKE documentation [Source: gcp/GKE/Explore GKE documentation > Core GKE documentation].

7. **Manage and optimize clusters:** Learn about administering clusters, including upgrades, notifications, and recommendations, plus node pool management for Standard clusters. This information can be found in the "Manage and optimize clusters" section of the Core GKE documentation [Source: gcp/GKE/Explore GKE documentation > Core GKE documentation].

8. **Observability for GKE:** Use managed Prometheus, Cloud Monitoring, and Cloud Logging to observe your clusters and workloads. This information can be found in the "Observability for GKE" section of the Core GKE documentation [Source: gcp/GKE/Explore GKE documentation > Core GKE documentation].

9. **Troubleshoot:** Find troubleshooting guidance and known issues for all core features. This information can be found in the "Troubleshoot" section of the Core GKE documentation [Source: gcp/GKE/Explore GKE documentation > Core GKE documentation].

e514db455cf212e8f3a50ec54b54d436737f7690.text:
Based on the provided context, there is no information about deploying a workload on Google Cloud Cloud Build. The context only discusses deploying workloads on Google Kubernetes Engine (GKE) using the Google Cloud console and Terraform.

To deploy a workload on GKE, follow these steps:

1. Enable the Kubernetes Engine API [Source: gcp/GKE/Create a cluster and deploy a workload in the Google Cloud console]

   - Create or select a project.
   - Wait for the API and related services to be enabled.

2. Grant yourself the Kubernetes Engine Viewer IAM role [Source: gcp/GKE/Explore your cluster and workload > Before you begin > Required roles]
   - Ask your administrator to grant you the Kubernetes Engine Viewer (roles/container.viewer) IAM role on your project.

3. Create a GKE Cluster [Source: gcp/GKE/Create a cluster and deploy a workload in the Google Cloud console]
